places.name: Alto Paraiso De Goias

visits.text: Leave Brasília early in the morning for the 230-kilometer drive north to Alto Paraíso—about 3.5 hours via BR-020 and then GO-118. The town sits precisely on the 14th parallel south, the same latitude as Machu Picchu, and has lived largely on spiritual tourism since the 1980s. Drop your bags at your pousada in early afternoon: the town center spans just two streets, but this will be your base for the days ahead. Fill up on fuel and withdraw cash as soon as you arrive—gas stations become scarce as you head toward Cavalcante.

Mirante da Janela offers a stunning view down onto the twin waterfalls of Saltos do Rio Preto—80 and 120 meters high, which you'll see up close the following day. The viewpoint sits on private property 8 kilometers from Alto Paraíso, accessible via a dirt track. Count on 2 kilometers of easy walking from the parking area. Go in late afternoon: the golden light hits the waterfall and canyon downstream perfectly. Bring a headlamp for the return if you linger until sunset.

Built around spiritual tourism in the 1980s, Alto Paraíso has kept its village heart. The Saturday morning organic market (if you time it right) offers cerrado fruits: pequi, baru, cagaita. Otherwise, wander the main street and choose one of the restaurants to try galinhada—the chicken-and-rice dish typical of Goiás—or peixe na telha, fish grilled on a clay tile. Several spots also serve vegetarian dishes, a legacy of the alternative community that settled here.

Head to Fazenda São Bento, 8 kilometers from Alto Paraíso on GO-239. This private property concentrates three major waterfalls and infrastructure rare in these parts: buffet restaurant, shaded parking, craft microbrewery. Combined entry costs 70 R$. Arrive at opening time (8:30 AM) to enjoy Almécegas I before the heat and tour bus crowds. Keep your ticket—it's valid for all three waterfalls today.

Cachoeira Almécegas I makes you earn it. From the parking lot, count on 1.5 kilometers of steep, rocky descent to a wooden viewpoint, then another 600 meters to reach the pool at the waterfall's base. The cascade drops roughly 45 meters down a quartzite wall, with water that stays ice-cold even in high summer. The return is a real climb—take your time, benches punctuate the ascent. Skip the flip-flops; several passages are slippery.

Jardim de Maytrea stretches along the roadside on the way to São Jorge. It's a wetland dotted with buriti palms—those trees with bright orange fruit you'll see everywhere in the cerrado—serving as a natural water reservoir for the entire region. Fifteen to twenty minutes is enough for the walk. Sunset over the buritis is worth catching. Local legend holds that the name comes from a spiritual vision an inhabitant had in the 1990s.

Vale da Lua isn't like other waterfalls. The Rio São Miguel has spent millions of years carving through carbonate-matrix conglomerate, creating craters, tunnels, and smooth bathtubs that resemble a lunar surface. The rocks range from 500 million to 2.1 billion years old depending on the layer. Count on 1.2 kilometers of easy trail from the parking area, then watch your step on wet stones. Entry is private, around 50 R$. Avoid the rainy season—flash floods are deadly.

activities.text: To get into the rhythm on your first day, head to Cachoeira dos Cristais. It's 8 kilometers from town center on private property, with an easy 700-meter trail. Quartz crystals scattered across the riverbed give the water a silvery shimmer. The pool is shallow, perfect for a quick dip after the drive. Entry runs around 30 R$. Arrive late afternoon and you'll have the place almost to yourself.

Before or after the mirante, settle onto the main street to sample local flavors. Pequi—a yellow, spiny fruit with a distinctive aroma—divides travelers: some love it, others hate it. Try it in arroz com pequi for a gentle introduction. Craft beers from São Bento microbrewery, brewed 8 kilometers from town, are served in several village bars.

On the way back toward Fazenda São Bento, take advantage of Poço São Bento. The trail runs 500 meters, flat, accessible to children. The pool is wide and shallow, with an 8-meter waterfall perfect for cooling off after Almécegas I. This is also where the fazenda's annual water polo tournament takes place. Buffet at the pousada right next door for 90 R$—wood-fired comida mineira.

If you still have energy late afternoon, Fazenda São Bento offers the park's longest zipline, the Voo do Gavião: 850 meters of cable, 100 meters high, at 55 km/h. Two positions available: seated (around 200 R$) or lying down Superman-style (around 250 R$). The activity runs weekends from 9 AM to 4 PM, or weekdays by reservation for groups of six minimum. Otherwise, head back to Alto Paraíso at a leisurely pace and treat yourself to a massage at your pousada—the local specialty.

places.name: Angra Dos Reis

visits.text: Angra dos Reis Bay is dotted with 365 islands—according to legend, one for each day of the year. This colonial port founded in 1502 by navigator Gonçalo Coelho was one of the most active on the Brazilian coast for trade routes between Europe and South America.

Igreja Nossa Senhora da Lapa da Boa Morte (1632) and Igreja Nossa Senhora do Carmo (1758) dot Angra's historic center. Praça Nilo Peçanha and its old buildings recall the era when the city served as a stopover for caravels restocking fresh water before the great crossing.

Ilha da Gipóia, the archipelago's second island, harbors beaches popular with families and a festive atmosphere around the floating bars of Praia do Dentista. Further south, Praia das Flechas is calmer, ideal for swimming.

Angra dos Reis Bay harbors one of Brazil's largest concentrations of shipwrecks, making its seabeds an exceptional dive site. Jorge Grego and Angra's Lagoa Azul dive spots allow you to observe multicolored fish, corals, and wrecks side by side.

activities.text: The ilhatour—a day-long boat excursion through the archipelago—is Angra dos Reis' signature activity. Swimming stops in the natural pools of Angra's Lagoa Azul (distinct from Ilha Grande's namesake site, deeper and flanked by cliffs), snorkeling on reefs, lunch stop on a white-sand beach, tour of private islands and their villas.

Hike to Banqueta waterfalls in Angra's mainland Atlantic forest: Véu de Noiva and Borboleta Azul falls succeed each other in a corridor of dense vegetation. Natural pools allow swimming along the way.

Hang-gliding flight over the bay: from Angra's heights, an aerial view of the island chain, the forest plunging into the sea, and Ilha Grande in the background reveals this archipelago's extraordinary scale.

visits.text: The Orla de Atalaia, a 6 km seafront promenade, is the beating heart of Aracaju. Cycling paths, monuments, sports courts and seafood restaurants line up between coconut and palm trees. Natural reef pools form at low tide.
The Mercado Municipal Antônio Franco, in the historic center, occupies a 1926 Art Nouveau building. Spices, renda lacework, artisanal cachaça from Sergipe and coalho cheese grilled on wooden skewers.
The Passarela do Caranguejo (Crab Walk), in Atalaia, lines up bars and restaurants specializing in crab with ice-cold beer. In the evening, the street turns into a party with live music and forró.

activities.text: Catamaran excursion on the Rio Sergipe to the Croa do Goré, a sandbar that emerges in the estuary. Lunch with your feet in the water, surrounded by mangroves and fishing boats.
Bike ride along the Orla de Atalaia at sunset. Kiosks serve coconut water and shrimp pastéis facing the ocean.

visits.text: Board a fast lancha in Barreirinhas for a downriver journey along the Rio Preguiças. The river winds between sandy banks and buriti palm groves before reaching the Pequenos Lençóis of Vassouras, the first dunes accessible by water.

The Farol de Mandacaru lighthouse rises 35 meters high on a sandy promontory. Climb its 160 steps: from the top, your gaze sweeps across the Rio Preguiças delta, the Atlantic Ocean, and the white expanse of Lençóis Maranhenses stretching to the horizon.

Caburé occupies a spectacular tongue of sand between the Preguiças River and the ocean. On one side, the calm, warm river waters; on the other, Atlantic breakers. Hammocks strung between palm trees invite a post-lunch siesta.

Atins, a village of 2,000 nestled among dunes, river, and ocean, retains its sandy lanes and low fishermen's houses. The atmosphere recalls what Jericoacoara was like twenty years ago: no paved roads, no crowds—just wind and silence.

Lençóis Maranhenses National Park unfolds across 155,000 hectares of white sand dunes as far as the eye can see. During the rainy season (January-June), valleys between dunes fill with turquoise freshwater lagoons, trapped by impermeable rock below. The Ponta do Mangue circuit leads to Lagoa Tropical and Lagoa Esmeralda, among the park's most crystalline pools.

The Canto de Atins circuit crosses an almost lunar landscape: wind-sculpted sand crests, crystal-clear pools where small fish swim, and not a tree in sight. The barefoot walk on warm sand between lagoons takes about two hours.

Atins beach stretches out facing the constant trade winds that make it a renowned kitesurfing spot. Sandbars sculpted by the Rio Preguiças form a natural lagoon sheltered from ocean swells—ideal for beginners.

activities.text: In late afternoon, head to the banks of the Rio Preguiças to watch guarás (scarlet ibis) flying over the river at twilight, crossing from shore to shore in crimson formations above the mangroves.

Try the famous grilled shrimp with molho secreto at Canto de Atins, the signature dish of the Lençóis Maranhenses region. The two restaurants at the point serve fresh catch of the day and carne de sol on terraces facing the dunes.
Climb the highest dune accessible from Atins for sunset. The white sand turns pink, then orange, while the lagoons below reflect the sky like mirrors.
Guided hike through the dunes and lagoons from Atins. A local guide knows the least crowded lagoons and adapts the route to the season's water level.
After the hike, relax in a hammock by the beach. Sand bars serve cajá caipirinhas and fresh coconut water facing the Atlantic.

places.name: Barra Do Garcas

visits.text: The Mirante do Cristo Rei overlooks Barra do Garças from atop the Serra do Roncador. The Christ statue — a modest cousin of Rio's — commands a 360° panorama of the confluence of the Rio Araguaia and the Rio Garças, the plains and the town below.

The Rio Araguaia, one of the great rivers of the cerrado, reveals white sand beaches (praias fluviais) from June to September when the water level drops. These sandbars in the middle of the river become swimming and camping spots.
The Parque Estadual da Serra Azul protects a patch of highland cerrado with natural hot springs, hiking trails and natural pools at a constant 38°C.

activities.text: Swimming and lounging on the river beaches of the Rio Araguaia. During the dry season, local boats shuttle between the sandbars that emerge mid-stream. Warm, gentle freshwater with no strong current.
Sunset on the banks of the Araguaia: the central-western Brazilian sky blazes with color above the river, with silhouettes of buriti palms in the foreground.

visits.text: Barreirinhas is the main gateway to the Lençóis Maranhenses, a 5-hour drive from Parnaíba. Arrival mid-day, quick check-in at the pousada, then first foray into the park in the afternoon. The town lives entirely off park tourism: 4x4 agencies, restaurants, buriti craft shops. Lively main street, but forget the "authentic village" aspect—it's a logistical base.

The Lençóis Maranhenses is your trip's visual shock. 1,550 km² of white dunes stretching for 70 km, dotted with thousands of turquoise freshwater lagoons. The sand is so pure it blocks infiltration, and rainwater fills the hollows between dunes—hence the name "sheets" (lençóis) referring to hung laundry. From June to September, the lagoons are full: that's THE moment. Lagoa Azul and Lagoa Bonita are the two most accessible from Barreirinhas.

The Rio Preguiças crosses Barreirinhas and flows down to the ocean. If your day is too short for the dunes, there's a lancha excursion on the river to Caburé and Mandacaru, two fishing villages between dunes and mangrove. At the Mandacaru lighthouse (32 meters), you climb 198 steps for a 360° view of the park—one of the trip's most striking viewpoints. Combine or alternate with the dunes depending on available time.

activities.text: Book a 4x4 with driver to reach Lagoa Azul then Lagoa Bonita late afternoon. The 4x4 drops you at the park edge, then you walk 15 to 20 minutes through the dunes to reach the first lagoon. Water at 28°C, white sand bottom, average depth of 1.5 meters—you can lie on your back without touching. Stay for sunset on the ridges: pink, orange, violet in less than 30 minutes. Unforgettable, and I mean it.

If you're leaving tomorrow, dine at a local restaurant in Barreirinhas. The Maranhense specialty is arroz de cuxá: rice with Amazonian sorrel leaves, dried shrimp, sesame. Strange, dark green, surprising. Restaurant Terral in Barreirinhas makes a good version. You leave with a taste that sums up your Northeast crossing: intense, unexpected, memorable.

places.name: Belo Horizonte

visits.text: Arrive in Belo Horizonte, capital of Minas Gerais founded in 1897. Transfer from Confins airport (40 km, 45 min). Drop your bags, freshen up, and walk to Praça da Liberdade. A good warm-up stroll.

Praça da Liberdade, the historic administrative heart, brings together a dozen early 20th-century buildings now converted into themed museums (Mineiro, Memory, Sciences). The ensemble is known as Circuito Cultural Praça da Liberdade, one of the densest cultural circuits in Latin America.

Drive to Pampulha, 15 minutes north. This modernist complex was commissioned in 1940 by the young mayor Juscelino Kubitschek from a team of artists, most at the start of their careers (Niemeyer, Burle Marx, Ceschiatti), with the notable exception of Cândido Portinari, already recognized as Brazil's greatest living painter after the 1939 New York World's Fair. UNESCO-listed in 2016.

Don't miss the Igreja de São Francisco de Assis at Pampulha. Vaulted reinforced concrete roof, azulejos painted by Portinari on the rear facade, interior decorated with a monumental Stations of the Cross. The bishopric refused to consecrate it for 14 years, deeming it "too modern." Open for worship since 1959.

Drive Diamantina → Belo Horizonte via BR-040 (295 km, 4h30). Breakfast stop in Curvelo. Arrive in Belo Horizonte early afternoon. Depending on your flight time, two options: drop bags at airport luggage storage or head straight to the final stop.

Late flight option: detour to Inhotim, an open-air contemporary art institute founded in 2006 by businessman Bernardo Paz. 140 hectares of botanical gardens (Roberto Burle Marx), 23 galleries dedicated to major artists (Hélio Oiticica, Cildo Meireles, Tunga, Matthew Barney). The world's largest open-air contemporary art museum.

If departing early evening, simply spend the afternoon in downtown Belo Horizonte. Rua Sapucaí in the Floresta neighborhood for street art, Praça da Liberdade for museums you missed on D1, Mirante do Mangabeiras for a final view.

activities.text: Dine at Mercado Central, the gastronomic soul of Belo Horizonte since 1929. Over 400 stalls, cachaça counters, mineira charcuterie, artisanal cheeses. Order the classic: pão de queijo + fígado com jiló (liver with jiló, a local bitter vegetable) + Boazinha cachaça. Lively, crowded atmosphere.
If the evening stretches on, head up to Mirante Mangabeiras for panoramic views of the illuminated city. The Belvedere neighborhood also offers excellent rooftops.
Lunch at Inhotim at one of its three restaurants (Oiticica, Bistrô, Tamboril). Contemporary cuisine, mineiro terroir ingredients. Count 80-120 BRL per person. Weekend reservations recommended.

Final pão de queijo of the trip at Confins airport. The Casa do Pão de Queijo chain has a presence there—you can bring frozen ones home in carry-on (insulated bags sold on-site). True enthusiasts complete with a bottle of artisanal cachaça at duty-free.

visits.text: Early departure Campo Grande → Bonito. 300 km via BR-060, MS-382 and MS-178. Entirely paved but minimum 4.5-hour drive with breaks. Open cerrado landscapes and pastures. Drop your bags at the pousada early afternoon. Warm-up afternoon to get your bearings.

Bonito, population 22,000, is the world capital of river ecotourism. The secret of its ultra-transparent waters lies in the geology: the Serra da Bodoquena is composed of limestone rocks that precipitate all suspended matter onto the riverbeds. Result: underwater visibility exceeding 50 meters in places.

The Gruta do Lago Azul, 19 km from Bonito, is the town's postcard image. Limestone cavity discovered in 1924, 100 m vertical depth. Descend the 200 steps carved into the rock to reach an underground lake with an intensely rare cobalt-blue hue. Prehistoric skeletons of sabre-toothed tigers were found here in 1990.

Return to Bonito late afternoon. Stroll along Rua Coronel Pilad Rebuá, the pedestrianized main artery lined with restaurants and tour agencies. Check your excursion schedule with your partner agency: vouchers are mandatory for all sites, strictly limited by daily quotas.

If time permits, swim at the Balneário Municipal on the Rio Formoso, 7 km from downtown. Developed natural pool, turquoise water populated by piraputangas (silvery fish emblematic of Bonito). Family facility with restaurant, playground, kayak rental. Admission 40 BRL.

First float: Rio Sucuri, 19 km from Bonito (Fazenda São Geraldo). After briefing and wetsuit fitting, short forest walk to the springs bubbling up from the ground (fervedouros). Mask and snorkel on, inflatable vest mandatory, and you drift 1,800 m on the slow current.

The Rio Sucuri is the third-most crystalline waterway in the world. Its name comes from its serpentine meanders ('sucuri' = anaconda, rare in the tourist zone). 2h20 float with support boat, observing silvery piraputangas, dourados, curimbas and emerald underwater vegetation. Depth 2-3 m. Touching the bottom is forbidden.

Lunch on site at Fazenda São Geraldo. Rustic but generous buffet, local products, dessert of doce de leite beaten in copper kettles. Figure 60-80 BRL with drink. Essential break before the afternoon activity.

Afternoon: Aquário Natural, 7 km from Bonito, in the Reserva Ecológica Baía Bonita. Short float (40 min) but spectacular in an area where piraputangas approach within 10 cm of your face. Ideal for families with children (from age 4) and for a first-time experience of the discipline.

Alternative for the more athletic: long float at Rio da Prata, 53 km from Bonito (in Jardim MS). 50-minute walk through the riparian forest (tapir, armadillo, birds) then 2,200 m float on the Olho d'Água and Prata rivers. The greatest number of visible fish of Bonito's four floats: piraperas, pintados, dourados.

Second day of activities in Bonito with several options depending on your preference. The must-do: Cachoeira Boca da Onça in Bodoquena, 56 km from Bonito. 156 m drop, the highest in Mato Grosso do Sul. 3 km trail, 800 steps, 10 other waterfalls along the route, swimming pools.

The complete Boca da Onça circuit takes 6-7 hours round trip (250 BRL with lunch). Trail with 180 m elevation gain. Add the optional rappel down the waterfall (90 m zipline descent, 150 BRL extra) for the more daring. Panoramic view over the Rio Salobra valley.

More athletic alternative: Abismo Anhumas. Vertical chasm 72 m deep accessible by rappel (15-min descent, 30-min mechanical winch ascent). At the bottom, underground lake housing 19.5 m tall calcite cones, the tallest known in the world. Optional float in the crystalline waters (adds 1 hour).

Less demanding option: Estância Mimosa, 24 km from Bonito. 4 km trail, 8 successive waterfalls in a canyon, swimming in turquoise pools. Family atmosphere, constant shade, less crowded than Boca da Onça. 220 BRL with lunch.

On the way back, detour via Projeto Jiboia. Educational center run by biologist Henrique Nicolete dedicated to cerrado snakes (boas, jiboias, jararacas). Demystifies local reptiles, supervised handling of a non-venomous constrictor, very educational and accessible to children.

activities.text: Dinner at a downtown restaurant. Casa do João or Juanita are solid bets. Pantaneira cuisine: grilled pintado (catfish), breaded traíra, banana farofa, palm heart salad. Local wine or Pacific beer. Figure 70-100 BRL.
Early to bed—6am wake-up tomorrow for the Rio Sucuri float. Activity departures in Bonito are strictly scheduled (7am, 9am, 11am, 1pm, 3pm slots) to preserve the environment. No delays tolerated.

Return to Bonito late afternoon. Recovery session at the pousada (hammock, pool). Quiet evening. Many pousadas organize a gathering around a campfire with grilled pantaneira cuisine (churrasco style). Book the day before, 80-100 BRL per person.

For insomniacs, brief observatory escape: Bonito's sky, free of light pollution, lets you see the southern Milky Way with the naked eye. The Southern Cross and Alpha Centauri are easily identified. April-September is the best period for observation.

Lunch at your chosen activity (included in most vouchers). Traditional pantaneira cuisine cooked over wood fire, arroz carreteiro (rice with dried meat), farofa, beans, free-range chicken. Products from surrounding fazendas.

Last dinner in Bonito. Try Vicio da Gula for revisited regional cuisine or Taboa Bar for cocktails with local cachaça. Final evening: pack a light bag for the Pantanal (3 days), main luggage stays in Campo Grande or Miranda depending on the agency.

visits.text: The return drive Cavalcante–Brasília runs about 320 kilometers; count on 4.5 to 5 hours leaving early. You'll loop back through Alto Paraíso then take GO-118 south to Formosa before joining BR-020. If your flight is late afternoon or evening, you'll have time for a half-day in Brasília. Otherwise, spend the night and catch your plane the next day. The federal capital, designed by Lúcio Costa and Oscar Niemeyer, was inaugurated in 1960 and inscribed on the UNESCO list in 1987.

If you have 2–3 hours on-site, head to Praça dos Três Poderes to see the alignment of Palácio do Planalto, Supremo Tribunal Federal, and Congresso Nacional with its two inverted domes. Next, the Catedral Metropolitana, completed by Niemeyer in 1970: its 16 hyperbolic white concrete columns support a roof of stained glass designed by Marianne Peretti. Inside, Alfredo Ceschiatti's suspended angels float in colored light.

Before the airport, make a final stop at Memorial JK to understand how Juscelino Kubitschek brought a capital up from red earth in less than four years, between 1956 and 1960. The president's mausoleum is displayed under a large glass cube, his tomb oriented toward Brasília. Thirty minutes is enough for the express visit. The airport is 15 minutes by car.

activities.text: For a final Brazilian meal before your flight, sit down at a churrascaria in the Asa Sul neighborhood. The rodízio—rotating meat service where you pay a fixed price and servers come to your table with skewers—is an institution. Count on 120 to 180 R$ per person. If you prefer something lighter, the food trucks at Pontão do Lago Sul serve good grilled fish with views over Lake Paranoá.

If your flight is very late and you want one last breath of modern architecture, cross the Ponte JK over Lake Paranoá. Inaugurated in 2002, its three asymmetric arches of 240 meters' span each reflect in the water. It's the postcard image you bring home from Brasília. Small parking at each end for photos.

days_plan.itin_title: Brazil Grand Tour: São Paulo, Iguaçu, the Northeast & Rio

visits.text: Arrival in Campo Grande, capital of Mato Grosso do Sul, nicknamed Cidade Morena for its characteristic red earth. Flight approximately 2 hours from São Paulo. Drop your bags at your downtown hotel and spend the afternoon getting your first impressions before tomorrow's big push to Bonito.

Founded in 1899, Campo Grande now counts 900,000 inhabitants. It's the agricultural crossroads of Brazil's center-west (soy, cattle, sugar). The cuisine blends Paraguayan, Bolivian and Japanese influences—the largest Okinawan diaspora outside Japan settled here starting in the 1910s.

The Parque das Nações Indígenas, in the city's north, is a vast 119-hectare green space laid out in 1997. Artificial lakes, running track, the Mato Grosso do Sul Contemporary Art Museum (MARCO), and the Memorial da Cultura Indígena dedicated to the state's 10 indigenous peoples (Terena, Kadiwéu, Kaiowá, Guarani, etc.). Free admission.

The Morada dos Baís, a restored 1913 colonial house downtown, contains a small historical museum and café-brewery. It's an excellent starting point for understanding the pioneer history of Mato Grosso do Sul, which separated from Mato Grosso only in 1977. Free admission.

Return Pantanal → Campo Grande. Early wake-up (6am), full breakfast, farewell to the fazenda. 4x4 transfer to Miranda (45 km via Estrada Parque, 1.5 hours) then paved BR-262 to Campo Grande (175 km, 2.5 hours). Arrival in Campo Grande around noon depending on return flight time.

If late flight (after 4pm), last stop at Mercado Municipal Antônio Valente. Built in 1927, it preserves its neo-colonial architecture and regional product stalls. Mineiro cheeses, cachaças, cerrado spices (pequi, baru, cagaíta), vacuum-packed dried meat (carne-de-sol) for the return. Guarani and Terena craft souvenirs.

The Memorial da Cultura Indígena, in the Marçal de Souza neighborhood, is a circular construction shaped like a Terena maloca (collective indigenous house) designed by architect Sinval Medina. Permanent exhibitions on Mato Grosso do Sul's 8 indigenous ethnicities. Free admission, closed Mondays.

Final lunch in Campo Grande. Casa Colonial serves upscale pantaneira cuisine (pacu in cupuaçu sauce, costela on open fire) from 120 BRL. More economical alternative: return to Feira Central for a last sobá. Then transfer to Antônio João airport (7 km, 15 min).

activities.text: Dinner at the Feira Central, one of Brazil's most original dining experiences. Founded in 1929, it brings together a hundred stalls where Okinawan Japanese and Paraguayans have created a unique fusion cuisine. Order the sobá (noodles in clear broth with pork and spring onion), the emblematic specialty. Family atmosphere, very gentle prices.

After dinner, a digestif at one of the bars on Rua 14 de Julho. You'll rub shoulders with people from Paraná, Rio Grande do Sul and Bolivia on the same street. Great spot to try an ice-cold tereré (the cold version of mate, served with lime juice). Early to bed—you've got an early drive tomorrow.

Last-minute shopping at the airport. Terena crafts (typical red pottery), tereré kit (gourd + bombilla), bocaiuva flour, pequi oil cosmetics. Pequi products are emblematic of the cerrado: beware the powerful and very specific taste (taste before buying in quantity).

Flight Campo Grande to GRU/São Paulo (2h) or GIG/Rio (2.5h), international connection. For combined stays, recommended extension: Bonito + Pantanal combines perfectly with Rio de Janeiro or with Iguaçu (2-hour flight from Campo Grande).

visits.text: Canoa Quebrada clings to ochre-red cliffs that plunge into the Atlantic. The village lived off fishing until the 1970s, when French hippies heading to India ended up staying. On the cliffs, you'll still find the large crescent moon and star carved during that era, now the village symbol. The main street, nicknamed Broadway, is pedestrian-only and lined with bars, restaurants, and shops. Walk it at sunset when the cliffs turn copper.

Praia das Fontes, 30 km back toward Beberibe, owes its name to freshwater springs that gush directly through the cliff face. You climb a trail along the ridge then descend at low tide to see the water bubble up from the sandstone. The nearby Mãe d'Água cave was a refuge for escaped slaves in the 19th century. Perfect stop on the route between Fortaleza and Canoa.

Aracati, 13 km from Canoa, deserves a 30-minute detour for its listed colonial center. Facades with blue-and-white Portuguese azulejos, the 18th-century do Rosário church, narrow alleys leading to the Rio Jaguaribe. It's one of Ceará's rare examples of colonial architecture, and nobody stops—you'll likely have it to yourselves.

activities.text: Take the classic buggy tour along the cliffs and neighboring beaches. The driver (bugueiro) asks "com ou sem emoção?"—answer "com" if you want the vertiginous sand descents and dune stunts. Three hours round-trip to Lagoa do Coqueiro and Majorlândia beach. Swimming stops, zip-lining over the lagoon, grilled fish lunch. It's the iconic Ceará experience.

If you arrive late afternoon, climb the cliffs on foot south of the village to watch the sunset. Praia do Amor stretches below, deserted. A few jangadas (fishing rafts with triangular sails) return to port, their silhouettes outlined against the horizon. This is Canoa's postcard image.

visits.text: Long drive this morning: 281 km from Feira de Santana to Canudos via BR-116 then BR-235, count on about 4h of driving. The landscape gradually shifts, vegetation grows scrubby, mandacarus appear, distances stretch. You're entering the deep sertão. Make a coffee stop at Tucano or Euclides da Cunha to breathe and break the journey.

You arrive in Canudos early afternoon. The present town is the third of its name—emancipated in 1985 under this name from the district of Cocorobó. The first Belo Monte founded by Antônio Conselheiro in 1893 was razed on 5 October 1897, the second founded around 1910 by survivors was drowned by the Cocorobó dam in the 1960s. During drought, the ruins of the first Canudos emerge from the reservoir.

Head to the Parque Estadual de Canudos, created in 1986 and managed by the State of Bahia. You'll find the Alto do Mário, the Alto da Favela, and the Fazenda Velha—the sites of the fourth military expedition's battles led by General Artur Oscar with roughly 10,000 men. This is where the war ended on 5 October 1897, after twelve days of intense bombardment with Krupp cannon and the 105mm Whitworth 'matadeira.'

The Instituto Popular Memorial de Canudos (IPMC), separate from the state park, preserves Antônio Conselheiro's Cruzeiro riddled with bullets during the war, a collection of popular art inspired by Belo Monte, and a dedicated library. It's the other major historical stop in town—more intimate than the park, and complementary.

activities.text: If it's the dry season and the reservoir level is low, go down to the edge of the Cocorobó dam. You can see the ruins of the first Canudos emerge from the water—foundations, wall sections, traces of Belo Monte's urban layout. Striking effect when you have Euclides da Cunha's 'Os Sertões' (1902) in mind.

In the evening, settle into a local lanchonete and strike up conversation with the inhabitants. The memory of Canudos isn't just textbook material here—it's family history for several lineages. Many have ancestors among the survivors, and the event remains a strong identity reference for the Bahian sertão.

places.name: Carolina

visits.text: You arrive in Carolina, your base camp for the next six days. The small town perched on the banks of the Tocantins River, at the border with Tocantins State, serves as the gateway to the entire chapada. Drop your bags, take a stroll around Praça da Matriz, and pass by the Igreja Matriz São Pedro de Alcântara. Spend the afternoon recovering, scouting local agencies (you'll need them for 4x4 access), and scheduling your passeios for the coming days. Book your National Park outings as soon as you arrive: group slots fill up fast during high season.

The Tocantins riverfront is where locals gather in late afternoon. The river runs wide and calm, marking the natural border with Tocantins State. Walk along the bank, sit on a bench, and watch the sun sink behind the distant mesetas rising on the horizon. A perfect teaser before the more spectacular landscapes awaiting over the next days.

Carolina's municipal market is the place to taste southern Maranhão cuisine. Bacuri, cupuaçu, cajá, buriti—the list of cerrado fruits runs long, and most will be unfamiliar. Also look for surubim no leite de coco and galinha caipira, two local specialties. Stock up on water and snacks for the excursions ahead.

You start the day at the Pedra Caída complex, 36 km from Carolina on the BR-230 (Transamazonian Highway). Spread across 12,000 hectares with six visitable waterfalls, a cable car, and complete infrastructure—restaurant, changing rooms, pools. It's the most comfortable introduction to the chapada, and the most touristy. The complex entrance has a fee, then each waterfall and activity requires separate payment. Arrive at opening to hit the falls before the crowds.

Cachoeira do Santuário is what you came to see: 46 meters plunging into a red sandstone canyon. You reach it via a wooden walkway of about 600 meters, then a section wading through water between towering walls. The roar, the moist air, the ray of light striking the falls mid-morning—the effect is immediate. Guide mandatory.

Within the same complex, Cachoeira da Caverna offers 12 meters of falls accessed through a sandstone cave. You enter from behind, through the water curtain, making it more playful than spectacular. The pool reaches 5 meters deep, with safety ropes installed. The 400-meter trail is on wooden deck.

You complete the classic Pedra Caída triangle with Cachoeira do Capelão. A gentler fall than its Santuário neighbor, set amid ochre-red cliffs. The name comes from the capelão monkeys that once populated the canopy above the falls. Ideal for catching your breath between more demanding trails.

On the way back to Carolina, don't miss the Portal da Chapada (also called Pedra Furada), 19 km from town. The window carved into the sandstone cliff rises about 76 meters and is reached after 600 meters walking through sand. Several viewpoints along the trail offer views of Morro do Chapéu, the Pilares da Chapada, and Serra da Cangalha. Aim for sunset—the low-angle light sets the rock ablaze.

Pre-dawn wake-up: you climb Morro do Chapéu at sunrise, otherwise the heat becomes unbearable. The trail starts about 13 km from Carolina, after 5 km on BR-230 then 8 km of sandy dirt road. Expect about an hour of steep ascent over soft sand then loose rock, with assistance ropes on technical sections. The summit is a broad plateau at 378 meters elevation—highest point in the chapada—from which you take in Carolina, the Tocantins River, Portal da Chapada, and Serra da Valença. Guide mandatory (the morro is in the national park buffer zone). Avoid rainy days: the sandstone attracts lightning.

In the afternoon, you head to Cachoeiras Gêmeas do Itapecuru, about 30 km from Carolina. Two parallel falls drop side by side into a swimming hole. The site has a restaurant overlooking the waterfalls and offers kayak rentals for paddling in the natural pool. A relaxed end to the day after the morning's exertion.

Today you truly enter Parque Nacional da Chapada das Mesas—159,953 hectares created in December 2005, at the meeting point of three biomes: Cerrado, Caatinga, and Amazonia. Very early departure from Carolina by 4x4 with accredited guide—it's the only option: sandy tracks are impassable without four-wheel drive. Expect about 30 km of pavement then 50 km of sandy dirt road, sometimes in poor condition, crossing private properties.

First stop on the route: Morro das Figuras, rock formations with strange silhouettes. According to ICMBio, human occupation of the chapada dates back over 10,000 years. The rock inscriptions on the small morro are sometimes attributed locally to Krahô populations (Jê linguistic family), though the attribution remains debated. Take a few photos before resuming the track—still a good two hours to São Romão.

Cachoeira de São Romão is the park's most voluminous waterfall, 25 meters high on the Farinha River (Tocantins tributary). You first arrive at the overlook from above, which gives the full measure, then descend to the natural pool at the base. A cave inhabited by swallows opens behind the water curtain—access possible depending on flow. The site includes a small restaurant, changing rooms, and kayak rental. Dry season (May-October) is by far the best; the river can become dangerous during the rains.

Sixteen kilometers from São Romão, you continue to Cachoeira do Prata. Two parallel falls of about 22 meters, which merge into a single water curtain during rainy season. A short trail with a small suspension footbridge leads to the river. The site is more for contemplation than swimming—the flow and rocks make swimming complicated.

You leave Riachão early morning to return to Carolina, about 100 km of road. The route passes back through the meseta landscapes you're starting to recognize. Make a stop on BR-230 to photograph Morro do Chapéu from a distance—its upturned-hat silhouette is recognizable from kilometers away and explains the name. Arrival in Carolina around noon, drop bags at the hotel, and lunch in town before the afternoon's passeio.

In the afternoon, head to the Tocantins River for the boat passeio to Pedra Encantada. The excursion lasts about 4 hours, departing around 3 p.m. You travel upriver 25 km through an area held back by the Estreito hydroelectric dam—calm, warm waters. Pedra Encantada is an 8-meter rock formation in the middle of the river, which the brave climb to jump into the water. The outing ends with sunset over the Tocantins, with the mesetas in silhouette.

To close out the trip, cross the Tocantins River by ferry (balsa) to Filadélfia in neighboring Tocantins State. The small river beach is where Carolina residents spend their weekends. Light sand, warm water, a few rustic bars—a quiet end to the journey before heading home.

activities.text: Planning your itinerary with a local agency is virtually mandatory here. National Park attractions (São Romão, Prata) are only accessible by 4x4 with an accredited guide, and roads aren't always signposted. Allow 2 to 3 hours to compare offers, confirm prices, and set your dates.

If you arrive in early afternoon, take time for a meal at a typical restaurant to discover sertaneja cuisine: carne de sol with macaxeira (cassava), fried tambaqui fish, rice and beans. A solid introduction before the athletic days ahead.

If you're craving adrenaline, the Pedra Caída complex offers a zipline 1,400 meters long with about 300 meters of vertical drop, reaching nearly 80 km/h. You ride a cable car up—about 400 meters of cable climbing to the top of the chapada. Separate fee from the complex entrance.
Take advantage of the complex's pools mid-afternoon to recover between waterfalls. The complex entrance includes access to pools, changing rooms, and rest areas—useful when the mercury tops 35°C.

Rent a kayak on the Cachoeiras Gêmeas pool to approach the twin falls from the water. The perspective shifts completely and you get a better sense of the wall heights. Allow 30 minutes to circle around at a leisurely pace.

Back in Carolina in late afternoon, head to the riverfront for sunset over the Tocantins. The red cliffs on the opposite bank catch fire. Many Carolinenses gather on the benches by the river at this hour—very local vibe.
Rent a kayak on the São Romão natural pool, about 30 minutes of paddling. It's the easiest way to approach the water curtain and measure the force of the falls.

Lunch at one of the park's small restaurants, usually next to the cachoeira reception. Carne de sol cooked over wood fire, rice, tropeiro beans, cassava—sertaneja cuisine served in a plank kitchen. Reservations recommended as facilities are rudimentary.

On the river passeio, jump from Pedra Encantada into the Tocantins if the urge strikes—8 meters above the water, the jump is clean and the pool deep enough. Optional activity; the boat stops at the base of the rock. Not required to enjoy the outing.

In the evening, final dinner in Carolina at a typical restaurant: fried tambaqui, surubim in coconut milk, or galinha caipira. Accompany with bacuri or cupuaçu juice, fruits you won't easily find elsewhere.

visits.text: The drive to Cavalcante takes 2.5 to 3 hours from São Jorge: loop back through Alto Paraíso, then take GO-118 south to town. The dirt track from Cavalcante toward the Kalunga community of Engenho II adds another 27 kilometers, in fair condition—count on an hour in a standard car, more in rainy season. Fill up on gas in Cavalcante and withdraw cash: Engenho II has neither ATM nor fuel pump, and some merchants don't accept cards.

Engenho II is one of 39 hamlets in Kalunga territory, Brazil's largest quilombola community. The Kalunga descend from enslaved people who escaped Goiás gold mines in the 18th century, took refuge in these isolated valleys, and lived there for three centuries in near obscurity—anthropologist Maria Baiocchi from UFG "rediscovered" them in 1982. The territory covers 262,000 hectares, designated Sítio Histórico e Patrimônio Cultural Kalunga by Goiás State in 1991, and the process for national cultural heritage designation was launched by IPHAN in March 2026. At the CAT (tourist center), you pay entry and hire a mandatory local guide.

The turquoise waters of Cachoeira Santa Bárbara alone explain why you made this entire drive. The waterfall stands 30 meters high, the pool 4 meters deep—learn to swim beforehand or rent a life jacket. The trail runs an easy 1.5 kilometers, after a 4-kilometer transfer in a local pau-de-arara cattle truck (10 R$ round trip). Arrive before 11 AM to catch the sun head-on in the water and capture maximum blue. After 2 PM, shade falls and the color dims.

An 800-meter walk downstream, Cachoeira da Capivara is less crowded but splendid: a cascade over 40 meters into a large, deep pool formed at the confluence of several watercourses. The trail descends steeply for 800 meters with ropes to help. The guide will explain that "Kalunga" comes from Bantu and means "sacred place, place of protection"—an etymology claimed by the community.

activities.text: Have lunch with a local family at Engenho II. Several families open their homes to visitors: everything is cooked over wood fire, most ingredients come from the garden or neighboring fields. Count on 25 to 40 R$ per dish, reserve through your guide early in the morning so they have time to prepare. Ask for the galinhada or grilled fish from the Rio Tocantins. Cerrado fruit juices (cajá, cagaita, pequi) are absolutely worth trying.

If you have time late afternoon, make a quick stop at Mirante de Nova Aurora, 6 kilometers from Cavalcante toward Engenho II. The panoramic view embraces the entire chapada to the south, and sunsets are spectacular in dry season, when the air is charged with golden dust. No entry fee, no facilities—just the viewpoint and silence.

days_plan.itin_title: Chapada dos Veadeiros: 6 Days in the Brazilian Cerrado

places.name: Chapada Dos Guimaraes

visits.text: Leave Cuiabá early in the morning to reach Chapada dos Guimarães, 65 km away via the MT-251 (Rodovia Emanuel Pinheiro). The road climbs gently through the cerrado for about an hour, passing the famous Portão do Inferno along the way—a viewpoint where the asphalt hugs an impressive cliff edge above the valley. The temperature drops by an average of 5 degrees compared to Cuiabá, and the air becomes drier. Drop your bags in the village center, which consists of a few streets around Praça Dom Wunibaldo, then grab a coffee before heading out to explore.

Véu de Noiva is the park's postcard image, and for good reason: an 86-meter waterfall plunging down the Rio Coxipó into a horseshoe-shaped rocky amphitheater where red macaws nest. The falls were called "Bocaina do Inferno" until 1928, when Archbishop-poet Dom Aquino Correa renamed them in a poem dedicated to the town. Access to the viewpoint is free; from the parking area, it's a 550-meter walk in full sun along an exposed trail. Swimming at the base of the falls is prohibited—the sandstone wall is too unstable. The park closes at 4 PM, with last entry at 3 PM.

Before heading back, make a detour to the Mirante do Centro Geodésico, 7 km from the center via a paved road. The sign claims it marks the "geodesic center of South America," equidistant from the Atlantic and Pacific oceans (1,600 km from each). In reality, the official marker is in Cuiabá on the UFMT campus, and Chapada's is just a leveling benchmark. But the view at 845 meters altitude is well worth the trip: on clear days you can see Cuiabá 30 km away as the crow flies, and the sunsets here are spectacular.

Back in the village, take a look at the Igreja Sant'Ana do Sacramento on Praça Dom Wunibaldo. A thatched chapel was built on this site by the Jesuits in the mid-18th century; after their expulsion, it was replaced in 1779 by the current church, with taipa walls and tiled roof. It's one of the few examples of baroque style in Mato Grosso state, classified as national heritage by IPHAN in the 1950s. Inside, Portuguese azulejos from the colonial era line the lower part of the choir.

Cidade de Pedra is only accessible by 4x4 with an accredited guide, so set off in the morning with a local agency booked the day before. Count on 10 km of asphalt on the MT-251 then 18 km of soft sand track—hence the need for traction. From the parking area, a 500-meter trail leads to the viewpoints. You'll discover sandstone walls plunging 250 to 300 meters into the Rio Claro valley, sculpted by wind and rain into shapes of needles, towers, and ruins. Access is limited to 120 people per day. Allow 4 hours on site, including viewpoints.

Along the way, plan several stops. You'll easily spot the nests of red-and-green macaws (Ara chloropterus) that take off in flocks at sunset from the rock walls. The rocks are sandstones from the Furnas and Ponta Grossa formations, deposited in the Devonian period about 400 million years ago, when the entire region lay at the bottom of a shallow sea. You can find fossils of marine invertebrates like brachiopods and trilobites. Local guides know the best photo angles depending on the time of day.

After Cidade de Pedra, descend into the Rio Claro Valley to see from below what you saw from above. Crista de Galo is a curious rock formation: its sandstone layers are vertical when they should be horizontal—a geological collapse likely caused by erosion of the base. The climb to the summit (50 meters of elevation gain over a short distance) offers a 360° panoramic view of the rock walls. Walk carefully—the path is narrow.

Head to Fazenda Água Fria, 40-46 km from the center via the MT-251 toward Campo Verde, for the cave circuit. Accredited guide mandatory, last entry at 1 PM, park closes at 5 PM. The round-trip trail runs 7 to 10 km depending on options: the path passes the Ponte de Pedra (a natural sandstone bridge) before reaching Caverna Aroe Jari after 1h-1h20 of walking. Along the way, look for the giant jatobá tree that takes at least five people to encircle.

Aroe Jari means "Dwelling of Souls" in Bororo, the language of the indigenous people who occupied the region before colonization. You enter the largest sandstone cave in Brazil, 1,550 meters long, whose entrance chamber measures over 30 meters wide. Inside, the temperature drops to 21°C—refreshing after the walk in the sun. The Salão do Chuveiro, an underground stream, and the Salão Dourado, where the ceiling glows like gold when the lamp illuminates it, are the two highlights. Headlamp mandatory, provided by the guide.

Coming out of Aroe Jari, walk 300 meters and you'll reach the Gruta da Lagoa Azul. A small pool of crystal-clear water reflects light on the wall in intense blue, particularly between 11 AM and 1 PM when the sun penetrates through the opening. Swimming is prohibited to preserve the optical effect: the slightest wave disturbs the coloration. Another 800 meters and you reach Caverna Kiogo Brado ("bird's nest" in Bororo), 273 meters long, fully passable with the guide.

activities.text: For lunch, settle into one of the typical restaurants in the center. Mato Grosso cuisine blends river fish, manioc, and cerrado fruits. Try the mojica de pintado (fish stew with manioc) or maria-isabel (rice with dried beef). Several places serve fixed-price buffets for around 50-60 R$ per person. Local craft beers are worth sampling—several microbreweries have opened in recent years.

If you arrive early and it's hot, Balneário Salgadeira is 15 minutes by car on the MT-251 between Cuiabá and Chapada. A waterfall spills into a developed natural pool, with a restaurant and picnic area. It's very busy on weekends with families from Cuiabá, quieter on weekdays. Entrance fee (around 20 R$), changing rooms and showers on site.

At the foot of the Crista, the Rio Claro flows between two rock walls in water so clear you can see fish several meters away. Ask your guide for mask and snorkel (often included in the price) and try the flutuação at Poço das Antas and Poço Verde. The current carries you gently along as you pass schools of small freshwater fish typical of the cerrado. The water is warmer than at the waterfalls, a rarity in the chapada.

In the evening, head to Mirante Morro dos Ventos, 5 minutes from the center. As its name suggests, the wind blows hard here, but it's one of the best spots for sunset over the rock walls. Several terrace restaurants take advantage of the view. The praça da igreja in the center sometimes hosts regional music concerts (rasqueado, siriri) on Friday nights.

The restaurant at Fazenda Água Fria serves a wood-fired regional cuisine buffet, designed to fuel hikers: braised meats, rice with bacon, black beans, grilled manioc, cerrado fruits in compote. Count on 50-70 R$ per person. This is also when guides organize groups for the return: on foot (3 km extra) or by tractor pulling a jardineira (bench cart), much more practical with a full stomach.

In the afternoon, on the way back, stop at Cachoeira do Relógio, a few kilometers from the fazenda. This small 5-meter waterfall takes its name from the fact that it's only lit by the sun after noon—hence the "relógio" (clock). The pool is wide and shallow, perfect for ending the day in cool water after the caves. Access is short from the parking area, 5 to 10 minutes of walking.

places.name: Coronel Jose Dias

visits.text: The Museu da Natureza, opened December 18, 2018 in the municipality of Coronel José Dias, is the park's other major scientific institution. Scenography by Marcello Dantas (creator of the Museu da Língua Portuguesa in São Paulo), 1,700 m² of exhibitions across 12 rooms: birth of the universe, formation of the Earth, cerrado megafauna (giant sloths, mastodons), arrival of humans in the region. The final film, screened in the last room, is narrated by Maria Bethânia. Open Wednesday through Monday from 1 p.m. to 7 p.m., admission around 30 R$.

At the museum exit, the mirante overlooks the park's sandstone cuestas—a breathtaking view, especially in late afternoon when the rock turns orange. A life-size replica of a giant sloth (up to 8 meters tall according to paleontologists) greets visitors on the way out. The project cost 13.7 million reais, financed by BNDES.

Head to Toca da Pedra Furada around midday. No confusion with its namesake in Tocantins: here we're talking about a sandstone formation sculpted by wind into a natural arch, dominating the valley of the same name. The site has essentially lent its name to the park—it's become the iconic image of Serra da Capivara. Short access trail, little elevation gain.

activities.text: In mid-afternoon, set out to visit Boqueirão da Pedra Furada itself, the great rock shelter that made the park known in the scientific world. The cavity measures 70 meters wide by 12 meters maximum depth and dominates the valley by about 20 meters. This is where excavations directed by Niède Guidon between 1978 and 1985, then by Fabio Parenti, uncovered traces of hearths and lithic tools studied in Nature in 1986. The stratigraphic sequence runs 5 meters deep, and the bottom contains dates exceeding 40,000 years BP—still debated in the scientific community.

Dinner and overnight in a pousada in Coronel José Dias or return to São Raimundo depending on your program. Coronel José Dias is tiny (under 5,000 inhabitants) and very quiet—a good option for those who want a night in a more rural setting, near the park, after four intense days.

visits.text: Cristalino Lodge nestles on a meander of the Rio Cristalino, in the heart of an 11,399-hectare private reserve of primary Amazonian rainforest. Accessible only by boat after an hour's drive from Alta Floresta, it protects six distinct ecosystems. Over 596 bird species have been recorded here.
The Ficus Trail crosses terra firme forest to a giant fig tree whose buttress roots rise several meters high. Brown capuchin monkeys and red-nosed sakis feed in the canopy above the path.

The 50-meter canopy tower, one of the tallest in the Americas, rises from the middle of the reserve. From the top, the forest stretches as far as the eye can see—an ocean of green rippling in the wind. Hawks, macaws, toucans, and colorful cotingas cross your field of vision. At sunrise, mist tears open over the canopy.

The Saleiro House, a wooden cabin perched in the reserve, overlooks a saladeiro—soil naturally rich in minerals. Tapirs, collared peccaries, giant anteaters, and tamanduás come to feed. The silent watch can last two hours before a rustle in the leaves betrays a visitor.

The Taboca Trail follows a seasonally flooded forest zone. White-whiskered spider monkeys—endemic species and emblem of the neighboring state park—travel in troops through the canopy. Bromeliads and heliconias carpet the damp understory.

The Sierra Trail crosses hilly terrain covered with semi-deciduous forest. Wild orchids cling to branches; scarlet bromeliads punctuate the understory. From rocky outcrops, the view plunges into the valley and canopy below.
The Cacao Trail follows an area where native cacao trees grow wild. Birds feed on the split pods. Anteaters, armadillos, and capuchin monkeys frequent this section, more open than the others.

activities.text: Evening boat trip on the Rio Cristalino. Caimans surface, capybaras graze on the banks, and tiger herons stand motionless. Guides spot wildlife with flashlights in the gathering darkness.
Swim from the lodge's floating dock, anchored in the crystal-clear waters of the Rio Cristalino. The rapids upstream offer an invigorating dip after forest hikes.

Dawn canoe on the Rio Cristalino. Paddles glide silently over translucent water as capped herons, kingfishers, and anhingas dry their wings on low branches. With luck, a giant otter crosses the current ahead of your bow.

Boat excursion to the confluence of the Rio Teles Pires and Rio Cristalino. The dark waters of the Teles Pires meet the clear waters of the Cristalino. Parrots, parakeets, and storks nest on the neighboring island. The sunset here is spectacular.

Second climb to the canopy tower, this time at dawn. The forest awakens in a concert of calls: the metallic song of the bellbird, blue macaws crossing the sky in pairs, multicolored-billed toucans emerging from branches. Low-angle light transforms mist into gold.

Picnic on a sandbank of the Rio Cristalino, followed by a swim in the rapids. Guides prepare lunch in the middle of the forest while blue morpho butterflies, electric-blue wings flashing, flutter between the banks.

visits.text: Before leaving Chapada, wake up early for the Circuito das Cachoeiras in the national park. The 6 km trail links seven waterfalls on the Rio Sete de Setembro: Cachoeirinha, Andorinhas, Pulo, Geladeira, Prainha, among others. Most are swimmable, some with developed natural pools. Registration at the visitor center is free but mandatory via the ICMBio website; guide not required. Count on 3 to 4 hours of walking, easy to moderate level.

You descend back to Cuiabá via the MT-251, about 1 hour down. The capital of Mato Grosso, founded in 1719 during the gold rush, deserves a few hours before your flight. Head to the historic center: the Catedral Basílica do Bom Jesus on Praça da República was rebuilt in the 1960s in a controversial style after the demolition of the colonial church. The praça remains the heart of the center.

To understand the region, the Museu Rondon (Museu de Antropologia, on the UFMT campus) displays a remarkable ethnographic collection of indigenous peoples of Mato Grosso: Bororo, Xavante, Karajá, Rikbaktsa. The objects were collected by the Indian Protection Service founded by Marshal Cândido Rondon, himself a Mato Grosso native, starting in 1910. Free admission, closed Mondays.

activities.text: For lunch or a last meal before the airport, the Mercado do Porto, on the banks of the Rio Cuiabá, serves local fish in popular restaurants. Pintado with urucum (orange-red seeds) and roasted pacu are the classics. Count on 80-120 R$ for two. Vendors also offer cerrado fruits and artisanal liqueurs (tucum, baru, cumaru). Noisy and authentic atmosphere—avoid Sunday midday (very crowded).

If you're combining with the northern Pantanal, now's the time to go: Poconé is about 100 km southwest of Cuiabá, the beginning of the Transpantaneira (MT-060), the 145 km dirt road that plunges into the world's largest wetland. Otherwise, Marechal Rondon Airport (Várzea Grande) is 15 minutes from downtown Cuiabá.

Northern Pantanal and Jaguar Capital: Cuiabá, Transpantaneira, and Porto Jofre in 6 Days

places.name: Cunha

visits.text: Cunha emerges from the mists of Serra da Bocaina at 850 meters elevation. Founded as Vila do Falcão in 1730 on the old gold route linking the mines of Minas Gerais to the port of Paraty, the town retains its character as a preserved Brazilian rural village deep in the Atlantic forest.

Casa do Artesão brings together local ceramicists around a collection of handcrafted pieces. Since the 1970s, Cunha has become Brazil's ceramics capital: a Pottery Route crosses the countryside, dotted with workshops where artisans still use the Japanese noborigama kiln technique, whose free flames create unpredictable color effects.

Parque Estadual da Serra do Mar - Núcleo Cunha protects one of the last remnants of cloud forest in Brazil, above 1,000 meters elevation. Its trails traverse century-old trees, springs, and waterfalls including Cachoeira do Barracão, nestled in a cirque of rocks and natural pools.
Cunha's lavender farm astonishes: rows of lavender, rosemary, and geranium spread beneath a Provençal-style house lost in the Brazilian hills. Artisan products sold on-site.

activities.text: Hike to Pedra da Macela (1,840 m): 2.3 km climb through forest, with 360° panoramic views over Serra da Bocaina, the coast of Paraty, and Ilha Grande in the distance. The border between São Paulo and Rio de Janeiro States stretches beneath your feet.
Visit ceramics workshops along the rural road. Rental shops offer bikes to tour the Pottery Route and stop at active noborigama kilns, where wood-firing produces unique pieces with each firing.

Swim at Cachoeira do Pimenta: a waterfall accessible on foot from Cunha's rural zone, surrounded by dense Atlantic forest. Natural pools carved by the current offer welcome refreshment after the hike.

map_keywords: Casa do Artesão céramique Cunha São Paulo
Parque Estadual Serra do Mar Núcleo Cunha

visits.text: The route from Tutóia crosses the Piauí-Ceará border, passing through Chaval and Barroquinha. The landscape evolves from coastal plains to low cliffs and coconut groves of Ceará's far west.

Praia das Curimãs opens onto a vast bay bordered by dunes and mangroves. Morning brings calm seas; afternoon trade winds whip up powerful waves. Fishermen's colorful jangadas strain at their anchors along the strand.
Curimãs is an authentic fishing community with virtually no tourist infrastructure. Low houses line sandy lanes, nets dry on the beach, and children play football at low tide.
Head to neighboring Praia de Bitupitá, accessible by beach or track: a wide band of fine sand, vegetated dunes, and a few artisanal fishing boats.

Spend a morning moving to the village rhythm: Curimãs fishermen depart at dawn and return between 9 and 10 a.m. On the beach, fresh fish is traded right on the sand in a lively, good-natured atmosphere.
Explore the freshwater lagoons that form behind Curimãs dunes at low tide. These natural pools, ringed by restinga vegetation, offer calm swimming sheltered from the wind.

Excursion by 4x4 or buggy to Camocim along the beach (about 40 min). En route, the coast alternates between low red clay cliffs, deserted beaches, and mangrove estuaries where fishing canoes are moored.

In Camocim, Barra dos Remédios forms a tongue of sand between river and sea, reminiscent of Caburé. Jangadas are moored along the bank, and Ilha do Amor, accessible on foot at low tide, offers an isolated sandbar in the middle of the estuary.

activities.text: Sail out with a local fisherman on a jangada for a one- to two-hour sea voyage. These traditional flat-bottomed sailboats glide across turquoise waters, cruising the coast from dune to dune.
Explore by 4x4 or on foot along the coastline between Curimãs and Bitupitá: isolated coves among mangroves, lagoons formed at low tide, and ocean panoramas from the dunes.
Go tidepooling at low tide on the exposed sandbars. Locals gather shellfish, crabs, and small fish trapped in pools among rocks and mangrove roots.
Kitesurfing session or beginner lessons on Curimãs beach. Consistent afternoon trade winds (15-25 knots) and the flat water between sandbars make this a favorite spot for practitioners.
Watch fishermen return and prepare grilled fish on the beach in late afternoon. Lobsters, mackerel, and red mullet are cooked over coconut shell coals, served with farofa and lime.
Sunset from the Curimãs dunes: the sky shifts from gold to fiery red as jangada silhouettes cut across the horizon.

places.name: Curitiba

visits.text: Curitiba's Botanical Garden, opened in 1991, fans out in geometric French-style beds in front of an Art Nouveau greenhouse of glass and steel inspired by London's Crystal Palace. The scientific herbarium holds over 330,000 specimens.

The Oscar Niemeyer Museum, nicknamed 'The Eye' for its giant eye-shaped tower, houses rotating exhibitions of Brazilian and international contemporary art. The sprawling sculpture garden surrounding it is a stroll in itself.

Largo da Ordem, Curitiba's colonial heart dating back to the 17th century, lines up colorful townhouses, the Igreja da Ordem Terceira de São Francisco (1737) and the Curitiba Memorial. On Sundays, the Feira de Artesanato fills the cobblestones with hundreds of artisan stalls.

Praça Tiradentes, birthplace of the city founded in 1693, is dominated by the Catedral Basílica de Nossa Senhora da Luz dos Pinhais, built in 1893. A stone obelisk on the square commemorates the city's founding under the Portuguese Crown.

activities.text: Parque Tanguá, a former quarry converted in 1996, features a 65-meter artificial waterfall cascading down a sandstone cliff. Climb to the viewpoint for Curitiba's finest sunset: the city spreads out below all the way to the Serra do Mar.

The Ópera de Arame, a theater with a tubular steel frame and transparent polycarbonate roof, floats above a lake in a former quarry. A walkway over the mirror-like water leads to the entrance. Jazz and MPB concerts echo from the floating stage.

Try barreado, the signature dish of the Paraná coast: meat slow-cooked for hours in a sealed clay pot, served with manioc flour and banana. Restaurants on Largo da Ordem also serve Italian and Polish specialties inherited from waves of immigration.

places.name: Diamantina

visits.text: Long driving day. Count on 5h30 behind the wheel for 395 km via Belo Horizonte (BR-040 then BR-135). Early departure recommended: 7am from Tiradentes to arrive in Diamantina mid-afternoon with time for a first exploration. Lunch stop in Belo Horizonte (Restaurante Xapuri or Mercado Central).

Diamantina, perched at 1,114m altitude in the Serra do Espinhaço, was UNESCO-listed in 1999 as a "remarkable example of human endeavor in a hostile environment." Founded in the 18th century as Arraial do Tijuco, it was the world center of diamond extraction under the Portuguese Crown. Entirely pedestrianized historic center.

First stroll through the steeply cobbled streets. White houses with colorful balconies, muxarabis (latticed balconies inherited from Maghrebi influence), and lajes de pedra pavements create a uniquely Brazilian urban landscape. Elevation varies 150 meters across the historic center alone.

Don't miss the Passadiço da Glória, a small blue covered bridge spanning the street and connecting two historic casarões. Now the symbol of UNESCO candidacy, it houses UFMG's geology center. Photo essential.

The Casa de Chica da Silva, Praça Lobo de Mesquita, is the former residence (1763-1771) of Francisca da Silva de Oliveira. Born enslaved around 1732, freed in 1753 by her master and companion, the immensely wealthy diamond contractor João Fernandes de Oliveira, she subsequently led a lady's life: she had her own church for Black people built, bore 13 recognized children, received a refined education. Now a museum.

Full day in Diamantina and surroundings. Begin at the Igreja de Nossa Senhora do Rosário, the town's oldest church, built between 1728 and 1731 by the confraternity of freed African slaves. Sober interior, painted choir ceiling, bell that has withstood three centuries of weather. Unique tower on the right side, rare in the region.

The Igreja de São Francisco de Assis, located on Praça Juscelino Kubitschek at slightly higher elevation, preserves in its nave the presumed burial place of Chica da Silva. Interior decorated with illusionist trompe-l'oeil paintings by José Soares de Araújo, rare in the region's mineiro baroque.

The Casa de Juscelino Kubitschek, Rua São Francisco 241, is the birthplace of the president who built Brasília (1956-1960). Traditional pau-a-pique house where he lived until age 18. The museum preserves personal objects, photographs, and correspondence. JK never stopped loving his hometown: he had a hotel, school, and faculty built there by Oscar Niemeyer.

The Museu do Diamante, housed in the Casa do Padre Rolim (one of the 1789 conspirators), chronicles four centuries of diamond extraction. Tools, measuring instruments, rough diamond collection, but also sacred art pieces and torture instruments used on enslaved miners. A raw testimony of colonial Brazil.

After lunch, excursion to Biribiri, 14 km north via a dirt road. Former textile industrial village founded in 1876, preserved in original condition. Waterfalls and natural pools on the Rio Ribeirão Pequeno. The Igrejinha de São João Batista (1876), a tiny pink chapel, is the site's postcard.

activities.text: The Mercado Municipal, known as Mercado Velho, on Praça Barão do Guaicuí, is one of Diamantina's postcards. Wooden structure from 1835 initially built as a tropeiros (muleteers) inn. Its arches reportedly inspired Oscar Niemeyer for the Palácio da Alvorada in Brasília. Market on Friday and Saturday mornings.

On Thursday, Friday, and Saturday evenings, the Vesperata takes place—a unique musical tradition. Musicians play from the balconies of colonial houses overlooking Rua da Quitanda while the audience dines at tables set up below. A magical moment typical of the town. Reserve a table in advance.

Lunch at a mineira trattoria in downtown Diamantina. Recanto do Antônio or Tragaluz Diamantina serve iconic dishes at fair prices. The typical jequitinhonha: arinto wood with sertão vegetables. Count 50-80 BRL.

Afternoon swimming in Biribiri's waterfalls. Multiple access points: Cachoeira dos Cristais, Cachoeira Sentinela, Cachoeira da Toca. Cool water (20°C even in summer), natural pools with polished rocks. Guide recommended from Diamantina (tour 150 BRL/person).

Last evening in Diamantina. If you haven't yet, enjoy the Vesperata or a simple dinner at the historic center's terrace tables. The local cachaça, Havana de Salinas, is produced in the Vale do Jequitinhonha 200 km north.

places.name: Feira De Santana

visits.text: You start in Salvador, pick up your rental car, then take the BR-324 northwest to Feira de Santana, 116 km away. Count on 1h30 on dual carriageway. Feira de Santana is Bahia's second city and the historic crossroads of the tropeiros who drove cattle from the sertões to the capital—hence its name, 'Sant'Ana's Fair.' This stop saves you from tackling the Canudos route in one push (another 280 km tomorrow).

Drop your bags and head straight for the Centro de Abastecimento, the city's big covered market. You're standing in the commercial heart of the sertão: carne de sol, queijo coalho, rapadura, farinha, dried fruit, medicinal herbs. Spot the umbu and cajá stalls—typical fruits you'll encounter further along. It's the best sensory introduction to the sertão before you dive in tomorrow.

The Museu Casa do Sertão, on the UEFS campus, is worth a detour. You'll find the material culture of the sertanejo: saddles, bridles, cangaceiro clothing, ex-votos, cordel instruments. Valuable context before Canudos. Open weekdays, closed weekends—check hours before you leave.

activities.text: Dine at a local carniceria or carne de sol restaurant. The specialty here is sun-dried meat, grilled and served with aipim (cassava) and farofa. It's uncompromising, and it's exactly what you need to taste as you arrive in the sertão.

Before heading to the hotel, spend 30 minutes filling the tank, checking the tyres, and buying water in quantity. Starting tomorrow, you enter the semi-arid zone: petrol stations grow scarcer and temperatures rise fast.

visits.text: The Mercado Público of Florianópolis, a market hall from 1899 in the heart of the historic center, overflows with fresh fish, seafood, spices and artisanal products. The counters serve locally farmed oysters with a squeeze of lime.

Lagoa da Conceição, a 20 km² lagoon ringed by lush green hills, is the bohemian soul of the island. Settled by Azorean colonists in 1750, it still has the Igreja de Nossa Senhora da Conceição and a small center of lanes lined with bars and galleries.

The Joaquina Dunes, a vast sweep of sand between the lagoon and the ocean, are the birthplace of Brazilian sandboarding. From the top, you can see the surf of Joaquina on one side and the calm waters of the Lagoa on the other.

The Ponte Hercílio Luz, a 340-meter suspension bridge from 1926 — the longest in Brazil — offers sweeping views of the bay and the city center. Long closed to traffic, it has been restored as a pedestrian landmark and symbol of Florianópolis.

activities.text: Swimming and surfing at Praia de Joaquina, one of southern Brazil's most famous breaks. Powerful waves draw international competitions. For calmer water, Praia de Campeche offers white sand and natural tide pools at low tide.

Hike to Lagoinha do Leste, a secluded beach only accessible on foot from Praia do Matadeiro. The 3 km trail winds through Atlantic Forest and opens onto a crescent of sand framed by cliffs — one of the wildest landscapes on the island.

In the evening, grab a table at a terrace restaurant in Barra da Lagoa for a sequência de camarão — a succession of shrimp dishes: grilled, breaded, in coconut sauce and moqueca. Fishing boats pulled up on the sand complete the scene.

days_plan.itin_title: Brazil Grand Tour: São Paulo, Iguaçu, the Northeast & Rio

visits.text: The Mercado Central in Fortaleza is a four-story cube planted right in the city center, just steps from the cathedral. You climb via helical ramps, and each level has its specialty: leather goods on the ground floor, colorful hammocks on the first (Ceará is Brazil's largest hammock producer), lace and embroidery on the second, cachaça and spices up top. It's touristy but vibrant, and prices are negotiable. Budget an hour, more if you fall for a hammock—bring a dollar per meter as a benchmark.

The Metropolitan Cathedral, right next to the Mercado, surprises with its sheer scale: 75 meters high, 5,000 seats, Brazil's third-largest church. Inaugurated in 1978 after more than forty years of construction, its neo-Gothic style with Romanesque touches contrasts sharply with the wooden stalls of the neighboring market. Modern stained glass in colored diamond patterns. Quick visit, but the juxtaposition is worth seeing.

The Centro Dragão do Mar de Arte e Cultura occupies a former port warehouse converted in 1999. You'll find the Ceará Museum, a cinematheque, and the Memorial da Cultura Cearense—this last one truly deserves the stop. It tells the story of sertão culture, the life of the cangaceiros (Northeast bandits like Lampião), drought, and forró music. The surrounding neighborhood, Praia de Iracema, becomes Fortaleza's nightlife hub after dark.

Praia do Futuro stretches for 8 km south of the city, lined with barracas—those massive thatched restaurant-pavilions that can accommodate hundreds of people. Chao do Mar, Crocobeach, Itapariká: each has its pool, stage, and vibe. Thursday nights feature the "caranguejada": all-you-can-eat crab while listening to live forró. The beach itself is windier and rawer than the upscale beaches, but it's the real seaside life of Fortaleza.

activities.text: Sample Cearense cuisine at a barraca on Praia do Futuro. The absolute specialty is peixada, a fish stew with coconut milk served with pirão (manioc flour puree) and rice. Baião de dois (rice with mung beans and fresh cheese) often accompanies dried meat. To drink: caldo de cana (sugarcane juice) or cajá caipirinha, made with the Northeast's tart fruit.

End the evening on Beira Mar, the long pedestrian avenue along the ocean. The night craft market, open every evening, lines up 600 stalls of hammocks, embroidery, sculptures, and leather goods. You can also grab a quick bite of stuffed tapioca or grilled churrasco. Perfect stroll for digesting.

days_plan.itin_title: Brazil Grand Tour: São Paulo, Iguaçu, the Northeast & Rio

Fortaleza, Jericoacoara & Gateway to the Lençóis: 7 Days on Brazil's Northeast Coast

places.name: Foz Do Iguacu

visits.text: The Itaipu Dam, built from 1975 to 1984 on the Paraná River between Brazil and Paraguay, is one of the largest hydroelectric plants in the world. Its 20 turbines generate 15% of Brazil's electricity and 90% of Paraguay's. The panoramic tour reveals the colossal scale of the structure.

Iguaçu Falls from the Brazilian side: 275 cascades spread across 2.7 km. A 1,200-meter walkway of catwalks delivers a sweeping panoramic view of the falls — 80% of which are on Argentine territory. UNESCO World Heritage since 1986.

Parque das Aves, just 100 meters from the national park entrance, is home to 1,300 birds of 130 species across 16 hectares of Atlantic Forest: toucans, parrots, cassowaries and macaws. Walk-through aviaries bring you remarkably close.
On the Argentine side, the upper and lower circuits of Iguazú National Park let you approach the falls both from above and from their base. Curtains of water frame the catwalks on every side.
The Garganta del Diablo (Devil's Throat) is reached via a catwalk above the river. A permanent cloud of mist engulfs the 80-meter-deep chasm — the most intense experience at Iguazú Falls.

activities.text: Arrive at the national park on the Brazilian side at dawn, right when the gates open at 7am, before the tour groups flood in. The first rays of sunlight strike the cascades and paint rainbows in the spray.
At the Marco Três Fronteiras, where Argentina, Brazil and Paraguay meet at the confluence of the rivers, three obelisks in national colors face each other across the Paraná. The best spot for sunset.
The Macuco Safari: a zodiac speeds up the river from the Brazilian side straight to the base of the falls, then punches through a curtain of water. Bring a change of clothes — you will be drenched.

visits.text: Galinhos stretches across a sand peninsula 500 meters wide, squeezed between the ocean on one side and an estuary on the other. With 1,200 inhabitants, the village is accessible only by boat or 4×4 along the beach at low tide.
Galinhos salt flats raise enormous white mountains amid the mangrove. Salt is extracted directly from seawater—Rio Grande do Norte is Brazil's leading salt producer.
The village of Galos, even smaller than Galinhos, is reached on foot or by donkey cart along the estuary. Its sandy lanes lead to a beach where you pass from river to ocean in a few steps.

At the peninsula's tip, the Galinhos lighthouse dominates a circular beach battered by waves. Wind farm turbines turn slowly in the background, sculpting a futuristic silhouette against ancestral sand.
Duna do Capim emerges amid lagoons like a miniature Lençóis Maranhenses landscape. Freshwater lagoons form between dunes after rains—their hues oscillate from emerald green to deep blue.

Navigate the mangrove channels by motorboat. The boatman plunges his hand into muddy water and pulls out a peixe-morcego, a flat-bodied prehistoric fish that's immediately released—a fascinating local ritual.
The salt flat mountain rises in dazzling white flanks above the green mangrove. Capture the right angle and the scene evokes a snowy landscape beneath the tropics.
Blue lagoons between the dunes form natural pools of warm water. Immaculate sand descends gently toward crystalline water—a bath worthy of a Polynesian lagoon.

activities.text: Village discovery by donkey-drawn cart, the only local transport besides buggies. Sandy lanes run past colorful houses—welcome to a Brazil beyond time.
Observe the salt flats and mangroves from the estuary. The vegetation shelters crabs, oysters, white and blue herons—and sometimes seahorses in shallow waters.
Sunset from the peninsula's natural pools. Warm water rises to your knees, the sky blazes—a moment of absolute serenity.
Half-day boat excursion: departure from the village, mangrove crossing, stop at the salt flats, swimming in Duna do Capim lagoons, and grilled fish lunch at Galos.
Buggy ride across the dunes to the deserted beaches north of the peninsula. Sand crunches beneath the wheels, wind lifts white swirls—a desert bordered by the Atlantic.
Meet local fishermen returning in their boats. Taste fresh oysters served waterside with a squeeze of lime—Galinhos gastronomy is experienced barefoot in the sand.
