places.name: Ilha Grande

visits.text: Vila do Abraão, Ilha Grande's only real village, opens onto a cove surrounded by jungle-covered peaks. No cars: dirt lanes, colorful pousadas, moored boats. The entire island has been a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve since 1992 and inscribed as a World Heritage Site in 2019.

Praia de Lopes Mendes stretches for 3 km of immaculate white sand, ranked among the world's most beautiful beaches. Count on 2 hours of walking round-trip from Abraão through Atlantic forest to reach it—the trail is part of the experience. Offshore winds and waves also make it the island's surf spot.

The Lazareto ruins testify to the island's troubled past: a 19th-century colonial lazaretto for sick passengers landing in Brazil, then a maximum-security prison closed in 1994. The ruins scattered in the vegetation are accessible on foot from Abraão.

Praia do Aventureiro, protected by an integral biological reserve, is one of the island's most remote beaches. Accessible only by boat or a 4-hour trail from Abraão, its calm waters, white sand, and forest plunging into the sea make it timeless.

Ilha Grande's Lagoa Azul—not to be confused with the namesake site in Angra dos Reis—is a natural pool formed between two rocks, with translucent blue waters, accessible by boat from Abraão in 20 minutes. One of the island's best snorkeling spots.

Saco do Céu—the Bay of Heaven—is an enclosed cove whose name comes from the sky's reflection in its perfectly calm waters. Surrounded by forested mountains and nearly closed upon itself, this bay is renowned for blazing sunsets.

Praia de Dois Rios is accessible on foot from Abraão via a 2-hour forest trail. This long isolated arc of sand borders the site of the former prison—foundations and some walls survive in the vegetation, recalling the island's decades of penitentiary isolation.

activities.text: Hike to Cachoeira da Feiticeira: 1h30 walk from Abraão through rainforest, crossing clearings and clear-water streams. The waterfall plunges 15 meters into a natural pool ringed by boulders and dense vegetation.
Kayak or paddle from Abraão: 20 minutes' paddling to reach Praia de Crena, where restaurants serve seafood caught that morning. The bay's interior waters stay calm all day.
Sunset from Pico do Abraão, a hill accessible in 30 minutes' walk from the village: panoramic view over the bay, forested peaks, and the lights of Vila do Abraão glowing at twilight.

Ascent of Pico do Papagaio (987 m): 4h30 round-trip from Abraão, 956 m elevation gain through jungle. Panoramic view over the entire island, Angra dos Reis Bay, and the coast all the way to Paraty. One of the Costa Verde's most spectacular treks.

Boat excursion around the island: stops at Ilha Grande's Lagoa Azul for snorkeling on coral reefs, swimming in the emerald waters of Saco do Céu, stops at deserted beaches. Sea turtles, multicolored fish, and dolphins offshore on good days.
Scuba diving from Abraão: Ilha Grande's seabeds shelter coral reefs, wrecks, and exceptional fauna. Jorge Grego and Lagoa Azul dive sites are accessible from the village in under 30 minutes by boat.

places.name: Itacare

visits.text: Itacaré, a former fishing village on the Cocoa Coast, sits at the mouth of the Rio de Contas, wrapped in Atlantic Forest. Its colorful streets, low houses and boats moored at the port recall the era when cacao made the region's fortune. The 'witch's broom' disease devastated the plantations in the 1980s, pushing the town toward surfing and ecotourism.

Praia da Tiririca is Bahia's best surf spot. Its powerful, consistent waves attract national competitions year-round. Beginners will find calmer water at Praia da Concha, a family-friendly beach with barracas and a panoramic viewpoint from Ponta do Xaréu.

The 4-beach trail cuts through Atlantic Forest from Engenhoca to Itacarezinho over 4 km. Each cove is a world apart: Engenhoca for surfing, Havaizinho for small reef-fringed inlets, near-deserted Camboinha under the coconut palms, and Itacarezinho, a 4 km arc of sand bordered by endless palms.

Prainha, reached only by a 40-minute hike through tropical forest from the center of Itacaré, is one of the most beautiful beaches in Bahia. The trail crosses dense canopy before opening onto a white-sand cove hemmed in by lush vegetation.

activities.text: Boat trip on the Rio de Contas: the river, born in the Chapada Diamantina, winds through mangroves and cacao plantations before emptying into the sea. Swimming stops at waterfalls accessible from the river, including the Cachoeira do Cleandro.
The Cachoeira do Tijuípe, 15 km south of Itacaré, tumbles through the Atlantic Forest. Its unusually warm water and wide pool make for a comfortable swim after the short access trail.
Beginner surf lesson at Praia da Tiririca or Praia de Resende. Local schools offer 2-hour classes with boards and wetsuits provided. Itacaré's waves are known for their year-round consistency.

days_plan.itin_title: Brazil Grand Tour: São Paulo, Iguaçu, the Northeast & Rio

map_keywords: Rio de Contas river mouth Itacaré

visits.text: Jericoacoara didn't get electricity until 1998, and the village deliberately keeps its rustic charm. No paved streets, no streetlights, no neon signs. The streets are deep sand—your shoes go in the bag as soon as you arrive. You can walk the entire village in 15 minutes, between Rua Principal and Rua do Forró. Wooden pousadas, passing donkeys, barefoot kids playing soccer. Drop your bags, stretch out, you've arrived.

The Duna do Pôr do Sol is Jeri's obligatory ritual. Every evening, about an hour before sunset, the entire village climbs this 30-meter dune just west of the last houses. You arrive breathless, the sand still warm underfoot, and wait. When the sun touches the Atlantic horizon, everyone applauds. It's cliché, it's touristy, it's also surprisingly moving. Bring a beer and stay after—the sky turns violet for a good 20 minutes.

At the opposite end of the village, 3 km east, Pedra Furada is the rock arch carved by the ocean that's become Jeri's symbol. You can reach it on foot only at low tide (check the tide tables posted everywhere in the village)—otherwise you get trapped in a cove. One-hour walk along the beach, with views of the Serrote cliffs along the way. Magical at sunrise when light passes through the opening.

Lagoa do Paraíso (officially Lagoa Jijoca) is Jeri's other iconic image: translucent blue-green water, white sand, floating hammocks installed directly in the shallow water. You reach it by buggy or 4x4 (45 minutes from the village). Several barracas share the shores—Alchymist and Rancho do Peixe are the references. Reserve a hammock, order grilled fish and a local kiwi caipirinha, and you won't budge all day. Water at 28°C, no current, feet in the sand.

Lagoa Azul, smaller and less crowded, sits 5 km from Lagoa do Paraíso. Even clearer water, calmer atmosphere, ideal if you want to escape the groups. Often combined in the same buggy circuit. A few more modest barracas, gentle prices.

Mangue Seco, 12 km east of Jeri, is a mangrove tongue where the river meets the ocean. At low tide, you descend the waterway by stand-up paddle or kayak, framed by the aerial roots of mangroves. Red crabs, herons, sometimes a curassow. Excursions organized from the village, usually combined with a swimming stop at a nearby lagoon.

activities.text: Try kitesurfing if you're here between July and December, the season of steady trade winds at 25 knots. Jeri is one of the three or four best spots in the world for the sport, and several schools offer beginner packages: 6 hours of lessons spread over 2-3 days, about 350 euros all-in. French-language instruction available. Thermal wind in the afternoon, perfect conditions.

Dine at one of the village's discreet gastronomic spots. Tamarindo, in a candlelit interior courtyard, serves Mediterranean-Cearense cuisine at a surprising level for a village at the end of a dirt track. Sky Restaurant, on a pousada rooftop, makes cocktails with cajá and tamarind facing the sunset. Book ahead, options remain limited.

Do the lagoon circuit by buggy: it's Jeri's classic full-day excursion. Departure around 9 AM, stops at Lagoa do Paraíso, Lagoa Azul, lunch on-site, return via the moving dunes at sunset with a stop at Pedra Furada if the tide permits. Budget 70 to 90 euros for a buggy with driver, split among up to 4 people. Ask for a bugueiro who speaks some English or French.

In the evening, head down to Praia do Forró for "capoeira na praia." Every evening around 8 PM, local capoeiristas set up a roda on the beach around a fire. Berimbau, atabaque drums, singing, and anyone can enter the circle. Not an organized show, no paid entry—it's just the village's nightlife.

visits.text: Depart Salvador → Lençóis. The Azul flight (3 weekly rotations, 1h) is highly recommended: 6h by bus via BR-242 in comparison. Horácio de Matos Airport, 20 km from downtown Lençóis, 30 min transfer.

Lençóis, founded in 1845, was the capital of Brazil's diamond boom. Its name comes from the white tents of garimpeiros seen from the hilltops, which resembled sheets (lençóis). Population 10,000, but the town was Bahia's third-largest at its peak. It has preserved its colonial street plan and multicolored architectural heritage.

Stroll along Rua das Pedras and Praça Otaviano Alves. Low pastel-facade houses, artisan workshops, former diamond merchants' mansions. The old French vice-consulate (1873), now the Subprefeitura, recalls when the town dealt directly with European buyers. Stop by the Museu do Garimpo for context.

Afternoon at Rio Serrano, accessible on foot from the center (20 min). Natural pools carved into red sandstone, polished stone slides, 2-3 m waterfalls. The water takes on a characteristic amber tint from plant tannins. Perfect swimming spot to recover from the journey.

Continue upstream to Ribeirão do Meio, famous for its 10-meter natural waterslide. The rock polished by centuries of erosion allows a cushioned slide into a large pool. Watch out for collisions with other swimmers on busy days. Municipal park entrance 5 BRL.

Big day as a classic "caves and Pai Inácio tour," the essential must-do excursion. Depart 8:30 AM from Lençóis by van with an agency. Route of 150 km total by car, 6 km of walking spread throughout the day. Return around 6:30 PM. About 200 BRL all-inclusive with lunch.

First stop at Cachoeira do Mosquito, 40 min from Lençóis. Short walk (20 min one-way) to a 25 m waterfall framed by ochre rocks. Swimming in the natural pool below. Then 800 m rocky trail to Poço do Diabo, another waterfall in a more dramatic canyon. Deep natural pool.

Gruta da Lapa Doce, 80 km west, is Brazil's third-largest cave (42 km mapped, 850 m visitable). The main chamber rises 70 m high. Stalactites, stalagmites, columns, calcite curtains, cathedral formations. Visit with guide and mandatory headlamp (45 min).

Fazenda Pratinha, a neighboring private property, features Gruta Azul (blue light reflecting the crystal-clear water at 2:30 PM in dry season) and Gruta da Pratinha, where a snorkeling float (40 BRL supplement) lets you explore 150 m underground. Transparent waters populated by small fish and bat nests on the ceiling.

Finish at Morro do Pai Inácio. 20 min ascent, 250 m elevation gain on rocky trail. The tabletop plateau at 1120 m altitude offers 360° panorama over Serra do Sincorá, Morro do Camelo and Morrão. The low-angle sunset light sets the orange cliffs ablaze. Absolute Chapada postcard.

Second major excursion: Poço Azul, Poço Encantado and Igatu village. Full day, 286 km of road, little walking but lots of driving. Depart 8 AM, return 5 PM. About 250 BRL with lunch and admission fees.

Poço Encantado, 150 km from Lençóis in the municipality of Itaetê, is a limestone cavity sheltering an underground lake 61 m deep. From September to March, between 10:30 AM and 1 PM, sunlight passes through a natural crack and illuminates the water in fluorescent cobalt blue. Swimming strictly forbidden to preserve the site.

Poço Azul, 20 km from Poço Encantado, shares the same light phenomenon with one major advantage: swimming is allowed. Floating with snorkel mask, mandatory vest, in 22-24°C water of absolute transparency. The golden noon light transforms the experience into a golden underwater ballet.

On the return, detour to Igatu, nicknamed "Brazil's Machu Picchu." Dry-stone village built in the 19th century by garimpeiros. Ruins of houses right in the village, picturesque cemetery, Museu do Garimpo installed in a former miner's house. Current population: 350 inhabitants.

In Igatu itself, the Ruínas do Garimpo, 15 min walk from the center, are the entirely abandoned former mining quarter. Dry-stone walls erected without mortar, remains of sorting workshops, cactus-lined paths. Desert-like and silent atmosphere, feels like a Western movie set.

activities.text: Dine on Rua das Pedras, the pedestrian shopping street in the center. Neco's serves a reimagined moqueca chapadeira (freshwater fish), Lampião offers a traditional Bahian menu. Count on 60-90 BRL per person with drinks.

Book your Vale do Pati trek for D5-D7 with a trusted agency: Chapada Adventure, Raízes da Chapada, Lençóis Trekking, Extrema Expedições. A briefing the day before is typically scheduled. Count on 1800-2500 BRL for 3 days all-inclusive (guide, family homestay accommodation, meals).
Lunch included in the tour, at Fazenda Pratinha. Simple but generous Mineiro-Bahian buffet (55-70 BRL). Wood-fired chicken, rice and beans, fresh salads. Good mid-route break.

Return to Lençóis around 6:30 PM. Dinner at a downtown restaurant: Cozinha Aberta (chef who worked in São Paulo, revisited chapadeira cuisine) or the classic Picanha na Praça for grilled meat. Quiet evening, prep gear for D4.

Lunch included at Igatu in one of the simple downtown restaurants. Flor de Açucena serves typical family cooking: feijoada, galinha caipira, homemade sweets (guava, banana, umbu preserves). Stone setting, warm atmosphere.

Return to Lençóis in the evening. Last evening before the trek: light dinner, early to bed, pack backpack for 3 days. The agency usually provides the list: technical clothing, rain poncho, headlamp, 2-3 liters of water, sunglasses, sunscreen, insect repellent.

places.name: Maceio

visits.text: The natural pools of Pajuçara form at low tide 2 km offshore. Colorful fishing jangadas cross the emerald water in 15 minutes to reach the coral banks where waist-deep crystal water reveals tropical fish, starfish and corals — a natural aquarium.

Praia de Ponta Verde, Maceió's main urban beach, unfurls its white sand along a coconut-palm-lined promenade. Its reefs also form natural pools at low tide. The Farol de Ponta Verde, a small lighthouse at the end of the jetty, offers a panoramic view of the bay.

The historic Jaraguá district, Maceió's former commercial port, lines up restored warehouses, art galleries and the Igreja de Nossa Senhora do Rosário dos Pretos (1867). Art Deco buildings on Rua Sá e Albuquerque stand beside colorful colonial facades.

The Feirinha de Artesanato de Pajuçara, a beachfront craft market, sells filé lacework (an Alagoas specialty), painted ceramics, embroidery and coconut products. Nearby barracas serve tapiocas and crab in sauce.

activities.text: Jangada trip to the Pajuçara natural pools: gliding over the corals reveals a multicolored seabed. The jangadeiro provides mask and snorkel, and floating bar-boats serve drinks and seafood right on the reef.
Sunset at Praia de Jatiúca, quieter than Pajuçara. Beach kiosks (barracas) serve local specialties: sururu (freshwater mussels), fried macaxeira and passion-fruit caipirinha.

visits.text: Behind the wheel early in the morning, head east on the TO-255 toward Mateiros, the tourist capital of Jalapão. The track is sandy, sometimes fine as flour - hence the 4x4. Before arriving, make the detour to Cachoeira da Velha, the park's largest waterfall: 100 meters wide and about 15 meters high on the Rio Novo. A well-maintained wooden walkway descends to several viewpoints facing the double curtain of water. Swimming is prohibited, the flow being too violent.

Just downstream from Cachoeira da Velha, walk 1.5 km along a forest gallery trail to Prainha do Rio Novo. It's the waterfall's reverse side: calm waters, white sand, small fish visible to the naked eye. Here swimming is not only permitted but practically mandatory after the long track from Ponte Alta. The cachoeira is located on Fazenda Triagro, and a tourist legend widely repeated in the region claims that Pablo Escobar used the location in the 1980s - no official source confirms it, but local guides willingly tell the story along the way.

Mateiros, about 3,000 inhabitants, lives from tourism and cattle ranching. The red-dust grid streets converge on a modest central square. Drop your bags at the pousada in late afternoon, take a walk through town, locate the restaurant where you'll dine (most close around 9 PM). Mateiros will be your base for the next three days - the position is ideal for exploring the dunes, fervedouros and cachoeiras.

Rise before 4 AM. The Serra do Espírito Santo trail begins in darkness: 500 to 800 meters of steep climb on sandstone, sometimes with ropes and benches to catch your breath. The equivalent of 80 floors of a building according to local guides. At the summit, the plateau opens onto a flat cerrado horizon, and just after 6 AM the sun emerges behind the chapadões. It's this serra that, through millennial erosion, provided the sand for the dunes you see below. Headlamp, water, windbreaker essential.

The Jalapão dunes extend over about 38 km² at the foot of the serra. The highest peaks around 30 meters, and the vivid orange color comes from iron oxides in the sand. Park at the base, climb on foot (10 to 20 minutes depending on your pace) and settle in for late afternoon. The park closes at 5:30 PM, mandatory exit before 6:30 PM. Note: no drinks on the dunes, no camping, photos only.

On the TO-255 road toward the dunes, Morro do Saca Trapo rises about 30 km from the center of Mateiros, adjacent to Serra do Espírito Santo. It's an isolated sandstone cone that juts from the flat - a classic photo on the car roof with the morro behind, and many visitors stop there. The nickname comes from a sack of old clothes ("saca de trapo") that old local hunters allegedly left at the foot of the morro. No trail to climb here, you stop at the roadside, look, photograph, and leave. A lanchonete offers drinks before the final stretch to the dunes.

Head to Cachoeira do Formiga, 35 km south of Mateiros on the TO-110. The pool measures about 8 meters in diameter and 2 meters deep, and the emerald-green water remains stable around 24°C year-round. The waterfall is only 2 meters high but its pressure is powerful: a true natural hydromassage. The bottom sand is white, visibility perfect. Bring a mask or snorkel, you'll see the small fish. Paid entry (about 20 R$), quilombola territory.

Fervedouro do Ceiça, 30 km from Mateiros, is one of the most accessible in the park. The principle is unusual: a water table rises through an opening in the sand, and the water pressure is such that you float effortlessly. No need to know how to swim. The rule is strict: entry in small groups, limited duration to avoid disturbing the source, no sunscreen or repellent (it pollutes immediately).

For comparison, stop by Fervedouro Buritizinho, smaller, surrounded by buriti palms. The sensation is the same but the setting is different - turquoise-blue water, visible roots at the bottom. A few minutes are enough to feel the upward push. These springs are fragile: walk on the walkways, not on the banks.

activities.text: For thrill-seekers, level-4 rafting starts right at the foot of Cachoeira da Velha and descends the rapids of the Rio Novo to Prainha. It's the angle the walkway doesn't show you. Count on about 2 hours including briefing and suiting up. Advance booking essential with a licensed agency.

Sample regional cuisine at dinner: galinha caipira (free-range chicken) with rice pilaf, paçoca de carne seca (dried beef pounded with manioc flour), beiju (manioc pancake). Many pousadas serve everything under an awning, no menu - you eat what's ready.

Sunset on the dunes is Jalapão's postcard image. The sand gradually shifts from vivid orange to dark red then to purple. Sit on the main crest, stay silent - the winds are strangely calm there, you hear the buritis rustling in the distance. No music, no drones, that's the rule.

Between the trail and the dunes, lunch in Mateiros and take a siesta at the pousada - the morning will have knocked you out. Jalapão's rhythm alternates physical efforts and long drives: save your energy when you can.

Between two fervedouros, picnic in the shade of the buritis. Many agencies provide a cold meal (sandwich, fruit, cashew juice) included in the passeio. Otherwise, bring your own - no restaurants between sites, just red track.

If you still have time in late afternoon, Fervedouro Bela Vista offers a nighttime session with lighting, usually reserved for Pousada Bela Vista guests. Cinema atmosphere, water still at 24°C even at night. Ask your guide about the slot.

visits.text: The descent from Serra da Ibiapaba to the coast takes about four hours. The landscape transforms: high-altitude forest gives way to the dry plains of the sertão, then to coconut groves and wind farms along Ceará's western coast.

Icaraí de Amontada (Icaraizinho), a village of 5,000, occupies a 3.7-mile bay between dunes and a river mouth. Sandy and cobbled streets, low houses without fences, and jangadas pulled up on the beach give it an end-of-the-world feel.

Praia de Moitas, 5 miles from Icaraizinho, is an isolated beach with pale sand and morning calm seas. At low tide, sandbars emerge to form natural pools. The atmosphere is that of a preserved fishing village, virtually without tourist infrastructure.

The Nossa Senhora dos Navegantes chapel, at the heart of Icaraizinho, serves as the village landmark. Around it, a handful of shops and restaurants form a peaceful centrinho, far from the bustle of Fortaleza or Jericoacoara.

The Rio Aracatiaçu winds through dunes and mangroves before meeting the ocean at Moitas. From Barra das Moitas, board a small boat heading upriver: the water shifts from ochre to emerald green as you penetrate the mangrove.

The Túnel do Amor is the journey's highlight: mangroves form a canopy over the river. At high tide, the water takes on a translucent turquoise hue and the canoe glides silently through a corridor of green. Crabs scuttle across aerial roots.
Ilha das Ostras, a sandbar in the middle of the river, is an unmissable stop. Mangrove oysters are harvested and shucked on the spot. You'll eat them with your feet in the water, facing the dunes.

The tall dunes of Moitas, sculpted by trade winds, offer panoramas over the river mouth, ocean, and neighboring wind farm turbine towers. Fine sand is dotted with coconut palms whose roots have been exposed by erosion.

Excursion to Caetanos, a village even more isolated than Icaraizinho, 12 miles to the east. The buggy route crosses a wind farm and passes a century-old cemetery buried by dunes—a surreal image of white crosses amid golden sand.

The Lençóis Cearenses, a miniature version of Lençóis Maranhenses, hide behind the dunes of Caetanos. From May to September, freshwater lagoons fill between mounds of white sand, forming warm pools bordered by low vegetation.

Praia do Jiqui surprises with its coconut palms whose roots have been exposed by coastal erosion and its natural freshwater spring (bica) gushing from the sandy cliff. The contrast between the cool spring water and warm sea is striking.

Lagoa de Flexeiras, 4 miles from Icaraizinho, is a translucent freshwater lake bordered by coconut palms and dunes. Barely three feet deep, the warm, still water makes it an ideal swimming spot for the whole family.

activities.text: Take an exploratory walk on Icaraizinho beach at sunset. Constant trade winds fill the sails of kitesurfers while fishermen sort the day's catch on the sand. Wind turbine towers stand in silhouette against the reddening sky.
Dine on grilled fish or fresh seafood at one of the centrinho restaurants. Dishes are prepared from the morning catch: grouper, shrimp, octopus, served with farofa and lime.
Take your first swim in the natural lagoon that forms at low tide in front of Icaraizinho: warm, shallow water without waves—an ideal stretch to unwind after the long drive.
Buggy excursion from Icaraizinho to Moitas along the beach. The route passes giant wind turbine towers, crosses stretches of deserted beach, and stops at a swing suspended over the Rio Aracatiaçu.
Navigate the Túnel do Amor by boat at high tide. The contrast between the cool shade of the mangrove and the turquoise glow of the water is striking.
Sunset from the Duna do Pôr do Sol at Moitas: the sun plunges into the Atlantic while kite wings punctuate the sky with color.
Hike or buggy through the Lençóis Cearenses. Between lagoons, the barefoot walk on warm sand recalls the Lençóis Maranhenses experience, with more intimate landscapes and without the crowds.
Swim in Lagoa da Cobra, one of the area's largest lagoons, surrounded by wind-sculpted dunes. The clear freshwater is warmed by the sun.
Kayak or stand-up paddle on the Rio Aracatiaçu, from the mouth heading upstream. The route crosses zones of silent mangrove, sandbars, and stretches bordered by coconut palms leaning over the water.

places.name: Monte Santo

visits.text: You leave Canudos on the BR-116 heading south, then branch off toward Monte Santo via Euclides da Cunha (formerly Cumbe, renamed after the author of 'Os Sertões'). Count on about 2h15 of driving. Monte Santo served as the main military base for the fourth expedition against Belo Monte in 1897—from here the troops marched on foot to Canudos, roughly 150 km away.

The Via Sacra de Monte Santo climbs 1,969 metres along the Serra do Piquaraçá. Twenty-five chapels mark the Stations of the Cross, created in the late 18th century by the Italian Capuchin friar Apolônio de Todi—one of Brazil's principal 'sacromontes,' listed by IPHAN in 1983. Antônio Conselheiro passed through around 1892 with his thousands of followers and repaired part of the steps—five years before these same places became the base of the army that came to fight him. Steep ascent, allow 1h30 to 2h return.

At the summit, the Santuário da Santa Cruz dominates the sertanejo plain. The main chapel preserves the images of Our Lady of Solitude, the Dead Christ, and Saint John the Evangelist. The 360° view sweeps across cerrado and caatinga: this is the landscape Antônio Conselheiro saw from the lookout, and it's in these expanses that he preached. The geographical effect hits you: you understand why the sertão was for him a land of election.

Back down in town, the Museu de Monte Santo occupies the former house of Coronel Galdino Andrade, from the early 20th century. Small collection but well curated on the tradition of romarias, Conselheiro's passage, and the city's history. Allow 45 minutes.

activities.text: If you're here on a Sunday or during Semana Santa, you'll encounter romeiros climbing to the sanctuary barefoot, sometimes on their knees, with stones on their heads. This tradition isn't folkloric—it's alive, and the All Saints' feasts (31 October - 1 November) draw thousands of pilgrims. Stay discreet, photograph respectfully.

Taste sertaneja cuisine in a restaurant on Rua Senhor dos Passos: bode guisado (kid stew), buchada de bode (stuffed tripe), galinha caipira, accompanied by a suco de umbu. These dishes are the staples of local cuisine, adapted to drought and the dominant goat farming.

days_plan.itin_title: Bahia Sertão - 5 Days Tracing Canudos, the Raso da Catarina, and the Rio São Francisco

map_keywords: Capela Senhor dos Passos Monte Santo

visits.text: The povoado of Mumbuca is located 35 km from Mateiros, on the TO-110 road between Mateiros and São Félix do Tocantins. The quilombola community descends from families who arrived from Bahia in the early 20th century - the parents of matriarch Dona Miúda (Guilhermina Pereira Matos, 1928-2010) came to the region around 1909. Today, about 165 inhabitants. The word "mumbuca" refers to a species of stingless bee, very common here, which gave its name to the place. Drop your bags at a local pousada or homestay accommodation.

The Associação Capim Dourado, founded in 2000, is the village's economic heart. About fifty artisans work "capim dourado" there - which is not a grass but a plant called sempre-viva (Syngonanthus nitens), endemic to the cerrado. The fine golden stems are sewn with buriti fiber to make hats, baskets, mandalas, jewelry. According to Dona Miúda's memory, the technique was transmitted in the early 20th century by passing Xerente Indians, who taught the already settled inhabitants to "sew the capim." Buy here directly from the artisans, not from resellers.

Fervedouro do Mumbuca, managed by the community, is one of the oldest to have been opened to tourism. Crystal-clear water, white sand bottom, palms all around. The upward pressure lifts your legs in seconds. The entry fee goes to the association - each visit contributes directly to the community.

activities.text: Participate in a weaving workshop with an artisan: you're shown how to sort the stems, moisten them, sew the first stitches with seda de buriti. Count on about 2 hours to understand the technique. You leave with a small handmade object and the measure of what a hat representing several days of work means.

If you're in Jalapão in late August or September, time your visit for the Festa da Colheita do Capim Dourado. Official harvest starts on September 20, preceded by a weekend of songs, dances and viola de buriti. It's the year's highlight for the community - don't photograph anything without asking.

days_plan.itin_title: Jalapão: 7 Days in Tocantins' Water Desert

visits.text: Genipabu dunes, 25 km north of Natal, form a massive shifting sand field in perpetual motion. Constant wind sculpts crests and valleys that change shape weekly—a miniature Sahara facing the Atlantic.

Forte dos Reis Magos (1598), a star-shaped fortress at the tip of a sand spit, guards the mouth of Rio Potengi. Built by the Portuguese then occupied by the Dutch, it recounts four centuries of colonial history.
Praia de Ponta Negra closes the circuit on an iconic note: Morro do Careca, a 120-meter dune covered with Atlantic vegetation, plunges directly into the ocean. This is Natal's postcard image.
Cross the Ponte Newton Navarro, a 56-meter-high cable-stayed bridge inaugurated in 2007. It spans Rio Potengi and offers spectacular views of Natal, the fort, and the northern coastline.

activities.text: Buggy tour through Genipabu dunes: the driver asks "com emoção ou sem emoção?" Those who choose "with emotion" hurtle down vertiginous sand slopes amid screams and laughter.
Swimming in Lagoa de Pitangui, a crystalline freshwater lagoon nestled between dunes and coconut palms. The warm, calm water contrasts with the nearby ocean waves.
Stroll along Praia de Ponta Negra at sunset. Morro do Careca blazes with orange hues while fishermen bring in their nets—the final act of a journey between dunes and ocean.

places.name: Nobres Bom Jardim

visits.text: Aquário Encantado is a 6-meter-deep basin fed by three springs from the Rio Salobra. The water, filtered through limestone, achieves total transparency. Over thirty fish species—red-finned piraputangas, golden dourados, cascudos—move between sand boils marking the springs. Touching the bottom is forbidden to preserve clarity.

Cachoeira Serra Azul, in the park managed by Sesc Pantanal, plunges 46 meters into a 30-meter azure basin populated with piraputangas. To reach it, count on 470 stairs down (and back up). A 700-meter zipline at 50 meters height offers a spectacular alternative to the staircase on the way back.

Refúgio das Águas, a few minutes from Aquário Encantado, conceals two natural pools fed by underground springs in the heart of gallery forest. The water is absolutely clear; the cool temperature contrasts with the cerrado heat.

Lagoa das Araras, 1 km from Bom Jardim's center, is a wetland bordered by buriti palms whose hollow trunks serve as nests. Every evening around 5pm, hundreds of blue-and-yellow macaws, maritacas, and parakeets converge on the lagoon in a spectacular revoada.

activities.text: Float down the Rio Salobra in flutuação (surface snorkeling) for 1,000 meters. The limestone gives the water transparency that reveals every detail of the bottom: schools of piraputangas, freshwater rays hidden in the sand, golden dourados. Life jacket and mask provided, guide mandatory.

Boia Cross at Duto do Quebó: descend 1,800 meters of river on an inflatable tube, alternating calm passages and rapids. The highlight: crossing a 330-meter-long cave, where your headlamp reveals stalactites, bats, and rock formations in total darkness.

places.name: Oeiras

visits.text: The Catedral de Nossa Senhora da Vitória, erected in 1733 on the site of a 1695 chapel, is the oldest religious building in Piauí. Its two asymmetrical towers — one topped with a bell, the other with a clock — frame a restrained baroque facade. The carved wooden altar blends rococo with sertão influences.

Oeiras, founded in 1712 as Vila da Mocha, served as the first capital of Piauí from 1759 to 1851. Its historic center, listed as a national monument, lines up colonial mansions with pastel facades, the Casa das Sete Janelas (House of Seven Windows) and the former governor's palace built in the 19th century.

The Museu de Arte Sacra of Oeiras preserves over 500 liturgical pieces from the town's three main churches: polychrome statues, silverwork, ex-votos and ornaments from the 18th and 19th centuries. Housed in the former town hall and jail, the building itself is worth the visit.

The Igreja de Nossa Senhora do Rosário, built in 1836 by the Visconde de Parnaíba, overlooks the Largo do Rosário. Nearby, the Casa da Pólvora (Powder House), the first military building in Piauí and only the third of its kind in Brazil, is a rough-stone arsenal still standing.

activities.text: Climb Morro do Leme, a small hill topped by a monumental cross erected in 1945. From the summit, the panoramic view takes in the entire town of Oeiras, its bell towers and the semi-arid caatinga landscape of Piauí.

Try sertanejo cuisine in the restaurants downtown: carne de sol (sun-dried beef) grilled over charcoal, baião de dois (rice and beans), paçoca de carne pilé (pounded beef with manioc flour) and cajuína, a fermented cashew drink typical of Piauí.

places.name: Ouro Preto

visits.text: Drive from Belo Horizonte to Ouro Preto via BR-356 (100 km, 1h45). The last 20 kilometers climb into the Serra do Espinhaço. Park at the Estação parking lot at the bottom of town. The center is pedestrianized and very steep: proper shoes essential.

Start at Praça Tiradentes, Ouro Preto's beating heart. It's dominated by the former municipal palace and prison of 1784, now the Museu da Inconfidência. The museum chronicles the 1789 conspiracy and houses the conspirators' pantheon in its crypt. Tiradentes, the movement's central figure, was hanged then quartered in Rio de Janeiro on April 21, 1792; his head was subsequently displayed on this square as a warning.

The Igreja de São Francisco de Assis, commissioned in 1766, is the absolute masterpiece of Antônio Francisco Lisboa, known as Aleijadinho. The rococo facade, soapstone medallions, and carved pulpits bear his signature. The ceiling painted by Mestre Ataíde depicts a mulatto Virgin surrounded by musician angels, an iconic work of mineiro baroque.

The Igreja Matriz de Nossa Senhora do Pilar, steps away, presents Brazil's most opulent colonial interior. Over 400 kilos of gold leaf cover altars, columns, and galleries. Red velvet curtains at the side balconies create a striking theatrical effect. The country's second-most gilded church after São Francisco in Salvador.

The Igreja de Nossa Senhora do Rosário dos Pretos, built in the 18th century by and for freed African slaves, breaks with conventional rectangular plans. Its unique rounded form and curved lines herald a popular baroque—less gilded but profoundly alive.

Second day based in Ouro Preto with an excursion to Mariana, 14 km away. Leave early to visit Mina da Passagem in the morning, halfway between the two towns. It's Brazil's largest gold mine open to visitors. Worked from 1719 to 1985, it produced 35 tons of gold.

A funicular trolley descends 120 meters underground into galleries carved in the 18th century by enslaved people. At the bottom, a translucent underground lake allows swimming. Guided 45-minute tour, equipment included. A striking immersion into the brutal reality of the gold cycle.

Mariana, founded in 1696, was Minas Gerais's first planned capital and the seat of the first mining bishopric. Its grid plan laid out in 1745 by military engineer José Fernandes Alpoim is unique in the region. Its main streets (Rua Direita, Dom Viçoso, Dom Silvério) can be walked in two hours.

The Catedral Basílica da Sé de Mariana preserves its German Arp Schnitger organ, gifted by the Portuguese crown in 1753—one of the rare instruments by this genius craftsman still in working order worldwide. Concerts on Fridays at 11:30am and Sundays at 12:15pm. The interior is stunningly rich, covered in gold.

Praça Minas Gerais forms one of Brazil's finest baroque urban ensembles. The Igrejas São Francisco and Nossa Senhora do Carmo, near-twins, stand in perpendicular tension around the Pelourinho (pillory) and the Casa de Câmara e Cadeia (1798). Inside Igreja São Francisco: paintings by Mestre Ataíde (who is buried there), soapstone pulpits attributed to Aleijadinho.

activities.text: Lunch on comida mineira at a pay-by-weight restaurant downtown. Tutu de feijão (bean purée with manioc flour), frango com quiabo (chicken with okra), couve (shredded collard greens), unlimited pão de queijo. Expect 50-70 BRL per person with drinks.

Afternoon: lose yourself in the steep cobbled lanes. Every turn reveals a new perspective: twin bell towers framed by green hills, colonial facades with wrought-iron balconies, plunging views over red-tiled roofs. Ouro Preto savors best on foot, slowly.

Late afternoon aperitif facing Igreja São Francisco with an artisanal mineira cachaça (Boazinha, Salinas, Havana). Ouro Preto is a lively university town: the repúblicas (student houses inspired by Coimbra) give weekend evenings a festive atmosphere.

Lunch in Mariana on Rua Dom Viçoso. Restaurante Lua Cheia serves pay-by-weight mineira cuisine in panelas de pedra-sabão (soapstone pots). Specialties: feijão tropeiro, frango caipira, sorvete de queijo com goiabada for dessert. Good value.

Return to Ouro Preto late afternoon. Pleasant option: the Trem da Vale connects the two towns Friday-Sunday in panoramic carriages (1h journey, 85 BRL round-trip). Online booking required. Departure times vary by season, check schedules.

Dinner in Ouro Preto. Try Bené da Flauta or Chafariz: revisited mineira cuisine with city views. Prices somewhat above average (90-120 BRL per person), but quality delivers. Weekend reservations recommended.

visits.text: Palmas is Brazil's last entirely planned state capital in the 20th century: foundation stone laid on May 20, 1989, seven months after Tocantins State was created. The avenues are straight, wide, numbered by quadras - a bit like Brasília, cerrado version. Take a tour of Praça dos Girassóis, one of the world's largest urban squares. At the center, the Araguaia Palace houses the state government headquarters.

35 km from downtown Palmas, the Taquaruçu district hides several cachoeiras in the mata. The best known, Escorrega Macaco, descends about 50 meters in two levels and ends in a pool ideal for swimming. Easy trail of about 1.5 km from the parking lot. The name comes from a local climbing plant said to be "slippery as a monkey." If your flight leaves in the evening, it's a perfect last swim.

Praia da Graciosa, on the lake formed by the Lajeado dam on the Tocantins River, is Palmas's urban beach: imported white sand, aligned kiosks, fresh and warm water. It's one of the capital's five official beaches, open year-round. Perfect for finishing the trip gently, after a week of tracks and dust. Local fish restaurants all along the shore, particularly grilled tucunaré and pirarucu.

activities.text: If your flight leaves in the evening, lunch at Praia da Graciosa with a peixe na telha (fish cooked on clay tile, local specialty) accompanied by farofa and rice. It's the Palmas version of the return-from-travel meal.

Stop by the Mercado Municipal for last purchases: pequi (cerrado fruit with powerful flavor), pamonha de Goiás, artisanal cachaça, and of course a few certified capim dourado pieces. Open early morning, quieter before 10 AM.

days_plan.itin_title: Jalapão: 7 Days in Tocantins' Water Desert

visits.text: Transfer Bonito → southern Pantanal. 220 km, 4-hour drive. The last 100 kilometers follow the Estrada Parque (MS-184), a 120 km dirt road parallel to the Rio Negro. Agencies generally offer transfers from Bonito or Miranda. Arrival at the fazenda early afternoon.

The Pantanal is the world's largest tropical wetland: 210,000 km² spread across Brazil (80%), Bolivia and Paraguay. UNESCO-recognized since 2000. Between May and October, the wet-season waters gradually recede, concentrating wildlife around the remaining water points. An open-air wildlife-watching paradise.

Welcome lunch at the fazenda. Traditional fazendas (Caiman, Aguapé, Barranco Alto, San Francisco) are former ranches converted into eco-lodges. Simple rooms or suites depending on category, full board included, 2-3 daily excursions with bilingual naturalist guides.

First outing in open 4x4 at sunset. The southern Transpantaneira or the fazenda's tracks reveal capybaras in groups (the world's largest rodent), jacaré caimans (population of 10 million across the entire Pantanal), marsh deer, mazama deer. Sunset is magical: orange light, silhouettes of cuba trees, toco toucan calls.

Full-day safari in three complementary formats: boat in the morning, guided forest walk midday, horseback or 4x4 at dusk. 5:30am wake-up, hearty breakfast (fruits, eggs, tapioca, pão de queijo) then 6:30am departure to enjoy the morning cool.

Boat safari on the Rio Negro or one of the corixos (water channels). 3 hours of observation from the water, silent motor, flat-bottom boat. Passing under flowering yellow and pink ipê trees, observing aracuã (noisy bird), green kingfishers, white herons, diving cormorants. If lucky, the famous pirarucu, a 2 m prehistoric fish.

With great luck, jaguar observation on a riverbank. The Pantanal harbors the world's largest population and biggest individuals (up to 140 kg). The central and northern zones (Porto Jofre) offer the best probabilities (95% in high season). In the south, observation remains possible but more random (30-40%).

Return to the fazenda for a late lunch and mandatory siesta (heat at its peak). Plunge in the natural pool. Passive observation from the hammocks: you regularly cross paths with capybaras nearby, toucans in the trees, and sometimes a gato-do-mato (ocelot) late afternoon.

Late afternoon horseback outing, quintessential pantaneira tradition. The pantaneiros (Pantanal gauchos) have been raising cattle in these plains since the 18th century. Peaceful 2-hour ride through flooded pastures, crossing corixos swimming. Accessible even to beginners.

Last full day in the Pantanal. Typical program: morning guided walk in gallery forest (accessible from the fazenda), return for lunch, specialized afternoon activity according to your wishes (piranha fishing, long horseback ride, targeted photo safari).

2.5-hour guided walk in the mata-galeria, ecotone between cerrado and flooded zone. The guide explains pantaneira botany: ipê amarelo, aroeira, piuva (the region's emblematic tree, spectacular pink flowering June to August), bocaiuva (palm that nourishes hyacinth macaws). Approach of animal tracks: jaguar, tapir, peccary prints.

Possible excursion to Fazenda San Francisco, 8 km from Miranda, specializing in last-chance night safaris for jaguar. Internationally recognized conservation program since 2003: every cattle loss to jaguars is compensated, eliminating revenge killings. Result: high feline density in the area.

Piranha fishing for enthusiasts. Emblematic Pantanal activity: hand line, red meat bait, in shallow corixos. The piranha caju is most common (40-50 cm maximum). No real danger to the fisherman (accidents are myths), but watch the teeth when handling catches. Grilled evening assured.

Late afternoon: final outing at sunset. The pantaneiros call this moment 'hora mágica'—the sky turns mauve above the palms, caimans climb back onto the banks in groups, migratory birds gather in tight flocks. A suspended moment of grace before departure.

activities.text: Dinner at the fazenda. Pantaneira cuisine: arroz com pequi (rice with the emblematic cerrado fruit), grilled pacu fish, garden salads, doces de goiabada-com-queijo (guava jam with cheese). Dinner is communal, excellent opportunity to chat with other guests and guides.

Optional night safari after dinner (9pm-10pm). Open 4x4 equipped with powerful headlights. Chance to observe nocturnal wildlife: giant anteaters, armadillos, pacas, porcupines, sometimes ocelot or tapir. Caiman eyes shine in the light, by the dozens. Supplement 80-120 BRL per person.

Observation of hyacinth macaws (ararauna) late in the day. The world's largest parrot (1 m wingspan), deep cobalt blue plumage, nests in the hollows of manduvi palms. The Refúgio Caiman fazenda hosts the Projeto Arara Azul which saved the species from extinction (numbers rising from 1,500 in 1990 to 5,500 today).

Second dinner at the fazenda. Pantaneira tradition: costela de javali (wild boar ribs) over wood fire, paçoca de pilão (pounded dried meat paste), tereré (cold mate) as an aperitif. Conversation with the manager and pantaneiros around the fire, jaguar anecdotes, viola caipira singing.

Farewell dinner at the fazenda. Some fazendas organize a fogueira (campfire) with the pantaneiros, traditional songs, demonstrations of laços (lasso) and doma (horse breaking). A rum infused with local cachaça and jabuticaba concludes the evening.

Tipping recommended for guides and staff (not mandatory but much appreciated): 50-100 BRL per person for the naturalist guide, 30-50 BRL for the service team, discreetly handed over the morning of departure. Prepare the cash beforehand—no ATMs on site.

places.name: Paraty

visits.text: Paraty's historic center has been UNESCO-listed since 2019. Founded in 1667 as an export port for gold from Minas Gerais, it holds Brazil's largest concentration of Portuguese colonial architecture. Four 18th-century churches, whitewashed houses with colorful shutters, cobblestone streets that flood at high tide.
Igreja Santa Rita, built in 1722 by freed Blacks, is Paraty's oldest church. Its white baroque facade stands out against palms and mountains. It now houses the Museum of Sacred Art.

Igreja Nossa Senhora dos Remédios (1787), Paraty's main church, overlooks Praça da Matriz, the heart of colonial social life. Its sober facade, twin bell towers, and baroque interior with gilded altarpieces speak to the city's opulence during the gold era.

The historic center's streets are paved with polished schist that reflects light. The colonial grid layout, flood drainage channels, and absence of motor vehicles make Paraty one of the continent's best-preserved historic centers.

Paraty Bay counts over 50 islands and 100 beaches accessible only by sea. Schooner excursions last 5 hours and stop at deserted islands with turquoise waters, notably Piscina Natural Caixa d'Aço, a natural rock formation that creates a protected lagoon.

Trindade, a bohemian village 35 minutes by road, is protected by Serra da Bocaina Park. A single road accesses it. Praia Cepilho is renowned for surfing, Praia Cachadaço for its calm waters bordered by dense forest.

activities.text: Visit artisanal cachaça distilleries on foot or by bike from the center. Paraty was the cachaça capital in the 18th century; some copper stills still run on local sugarcane. The cinnamon and clove variety is the region's signature.

Kayak Jabaquara Bay in late afternoon: golden light on the islands and mangroves is particularly beautiful. Circle tropical islets, navigate mangrove corridors, reach deserted beaches accessible only by sea.
Dinner in the historic center at sunset. Tables spill onto cobblestone streets, candles glow in colonial windows. Fresh fish of the day, coconut moqueca, artisanal caipirinha.

Schooner excursion in the bay: early morning departure from the port, five hours among deserted islands. The captain anchors at snorkeling spots, where the seabed reveals turtles and tropical fish. Onboard: caipirinhas and fresh fruit.
Natural slide at Penha, 15 km from the center: a rock polished smooth by the current where you glide into a fresh-water pool deep in the Atlantic forest.
Hike in Serra da Bocaina from Paraty's heights: one of Brazil's best-preserved Atlantic forests. Trails lead to hidden waterfalls and offer ocean and island views in the distance.

places.name: Paraty Centro Historico

visits.text: Paraty's historic center has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2019. Founded in 1667 as the export port for Minas Gerais gold, it's the finest concentration of Portuguese colonial architecture in Brazil. Four 18th-century churches, whitewashed houses with colorful shutters, cobblestone streets that flood at high tide.
Igreja Santa Rita, built in 1722 by freed Africans, is Paraty's oldest church. Its white baroque façade stands out against the palms and mountains. Today it houses the Museum of Sacred Art.
Trindade, a bohemian village 35 minutes from Paraty, is protected by Serra da Bocaina Park. A single road leads in. Praia Cepilho is renowned for surf, Praia Cachadaço for calm, crystal-clear waters.

Paraty Bay harbors over 50 islands and 100 beaches accessible only by sea. Boat tours (escuna) last 5 hours and stop at deserted islands with turquoise waters: Ilha Comprida and the natural swimming pool Caixa d'Aço, a rock formation sculpted by nature.

The Penha Natural Waterslide, 20 minutes from the center, is a 15-meter smooth rock slide where river water propels you into a natural pool surrounded by Atlantic rainforest. Local fishermen adjust the water flow to control your speed.

activities.text: Visit artisanal cachaça distilleries on foot or by bike from the center. Paraty was Brazil's cachaça capital in the 18th century.

Wander the art galleries and cobbled lanes of the historic center in the late afternoon. Colonial houses shelter painting studios, artisan jewelers, and independent bookshops. At high tide, seawater floods the streets—a spectacle unique to Paraty.
Kayak around Jabaquara Bay: navigate tropical islands, glide through mangroves, reach deserted beaches at your own pace.

visits.text: The Jeri-Parnaíba crossing by 4x4 is an adventure in itself: 6 hours of dirt tracks, beaches, and river crossings at low tide. You leave Jeri around 7 AM, first stop at Tatajuba (1 hour drive)—a village that was buried by moving dunes in 1990 and rebuilt 5 km away. The old church bell tower still emerges from the sand sometimes. Obligatory photo, swim in nearby Lagoa Torta.

You then cross several small ferries to ford the rivers between Tatajuba and Camocim, then follow deserted beaches to Barra Grande, a kitesurf station in Piauí. One-hour lunch stop, swimming. The track then continues through Piauí dunes to Parnaíba, your overnight stop. It's exhausting, bumpy, magnificent. Stay well hydrated, bring a scarf against the sand.

Parnaíba itself is a stopover town without great tourist appeal, but Porto dos Tatus, 12 km from the center, deserves an evening look. This is where the lanchas depart for the delta. Small wooden port on stilts, fish shacks, moored jangadas. You can already breathe the atmosphere of tomorrow's adventure.

The Parnaíba Delta is one of only three open-ocean deltas in the world (along with the Nile and Mekong) and the only one in the Americas. 2,700 km², 75 islands, a labyrinth of waterways lined with mangroves, straddling Piauí and Maranhão. Departure by lancha (fast boat) at 8 AM from Porto dos Tatus, full day on the water. Stops to observe guarás (scarlet ibis) at sunset, swimming in an interior lagoon, grilled fish lunch on Morro do Meio island.

Ilha do Caju, in the Maranhão part of the delta, is a private 100 km² reserve belonging to the same family since 1847. You'll find about ten different ecosystems and a superb ecolodge for those who want to extend their stay. Group tours don't all stop here—specify if you want to see it, on a private tour. Manatees, armadillos, alligators in the interior rivers.

Before returning, operators often make a final stop on the sandbars that emerge at low tide right in the middle of the delta. Several hectares of immaculate white sand that will disappear under water in a few hours. The feeling of being on a floating beach, golden light on the return. Get your camera ready.

activities.text: If you arrive early enough in Parnaíba, dine at Coco Bambu restaurant by the river: grilled tilapia, delta lobster, açaí for dessert. Get to bed early—the lancha for the delta leaves at 8 AM tomorrow and the night was short.

If the fatigue from the crossing still leaves you with energy, swim at sunset at Praia da Pedra do Sal, 15 km from Parnaíba. Fairly wild beach in Piauí state, red cliffs and interior lagoon. Few people even in high season.

Taste the delta's blue crab (caranguejo-uçá) on your return. Several barracas at Porto dos Tatus serve it still live, freshly shelled, with a sauce of coconut milk and cow's milk (the "caranguejo toada"). It's messy, you eat with your fingers, you emerge as happy diners. Count on a good hour.

For those with time before nightfall, go birdwatching in the mangroves at dusk by kayak. Several delta ecolodges organize this outing as a complement to the lancha tour. You glide silently between the aerial roots, and scarlet ibis arrive by the hundreds for the night. One of the trip's most memorable images.

places.name: Paulo Afonso

visits.text: Long drive from Monte Santo to Paulo Afonso, roughly 280 km via BR-116 then BR-110, count on 4h30 with stops. To break the journey, stop at Jeremoabo, about 100 km from Paulo Afonso: it's one of the municipalities from where Lear's macaws are observed and the seat of the 'Programa de Conservação da Arara-Azul-de-Lear' created in 2001.

If your schedule allows, the Estação Biológica de Canudos, managed by the Fundação Biodiversitas (130 hectares acquired in 1993, expanded to 1,500 hectares in 2007), is one of the rare sites where visitors can observe Lear's macaws nesting in the sandstone cliffs. The species, endemic to the caatinga of northeastern Bahia, was rediscovered in 1978 by ornithologist Helmut Sick—it had been considered lost since its description by Bonaparte in 1856. Wild population estimated at around 2,000 individuals. Booking required in advance.

The Raso da Catarina protected area itself, instituted as an ecological reserve by decree 89.268 of 3 January 1984 then reclassified as Estação Ecológica in 2001, covers roughly 105,000 hectares of shrubby caatinga between the rios São Francisco and Vaza-Barris. Entry is forbidden to the public outside scientific research. What you can do: drive along the BR-110 between Paulo Afonso and Jeremoabo, observe the landscapes from the buffer zone, and—with a local guide—access certain viewpoints over the sandstone cliffs, refuge of cangaceiros in the early 20th century, including Lampião.

You arrive in Paulo Afonso late afternoon. The town owes its name to Paulo Viveiros Afonso, sertanista who received a sesmaria in 1725 including the cachoeira. Before 1948, it was a dusty village—the creation of CHESF (Companhia Hidrelétrica do São Francisco) that year transformed everything. The historic cachoeira, which impressed Dom Pedro II in 1859, is now dry because of the dams, except during exceptional floods.

You cross the Ponte Metálica into Alagoas, then follow the south bank to Canindé do São Francisco (Sergipe), about 65 km from Paulo Afonso. This is where the catamarans embark for the Xingó canyon. The Xingó dam, closed on 10 June 1994, drowned the river's old corredeiras and created a 65 km reservoir, navigable to Paulo Afonso. Depth up to 180 metres between the granite cliffs.

Board for the canyon cruise. Count on roughly 2h30 of navigation through what's billed as 'the world's 5th navigable canyon'—local marketing formula, but the scenery delivers: emerald waters, orange walls, sparse caatinga atop the cliffs. Chance to swim in certain coves, and close passage of the caves known as Furnas do Morcego, which sheltered Lampião and his bando in the 1930s.

In Canindé, don't miss the Museu de Arqueologia de Xingó (MAX), managed by the Universidade Federal de Sergipe. Created in 2000, it preserves the archaeological material saved before the dam's flooding: ceramics, burials, lithic tools of prehistoric populations of the São Francisco valley. Scientific perspective on everything you've just seen disappear beneath the waters.

activities.text: Dine facing the Rio São Francisco. The cuisine of the 'Velho Chico' (Old Frankie) revolves around freshwater fish: surubim, pintado, dourado. Pintado na telha, cooked on tile in its juice, is a classic. Also taste umbu juice, king fruit of the sertão, and local cachaça.

Walk across the Ponte Metálica Dom Pedro II, 1950s ironwork on the BR-110 that links Paulo Afonso (Bahia) to Delmiro Gouveia (Alagoas). It marks the start of the São Francisco canyon and offers a plunging view over the first granite cliffs, up to roughly 50 metres above the water. It's also kilometre zero of the Xingó reservoir.

To finish the trip, cross over to Piranhas in Alagoas, small listed colonial port on the opposite bank, historic base of Lampião's bando. The town preserves its 19th-century centre and offers 'Rota do Cangaço' tours that retrace the famous cangaceiro's end at Angicos in 1938. Count 1h30 on site before heading back to Paulo Afonso for the night, or pushing on to Aracaju (200 km) if your flight leaves from there.

Final regional lunch at the port of Canindé: moqueca de surubim, sopa de pirarucu, or grilled pintado. The riverside restaurants serve fish caught that same morning. Close the loop with a coffee and a rapadura from the region.

places.name: Petropolis

visits.text: The Imperial Museum occupies Dom Pedro II's summer palace, built in 1845 in neoclassical style at the heart of the city he founded by decree in 1843. Polished parquet floors, music salons, Sèvres porcelain, and the imperial crown adorned with 639 diamonds are presented as they were in Empire times. The visit is conducted in slippers provided at the entrance.

Cathedral São Pedro de Alcântara is a neo-Gothic edifice completed in 1925. Its stained glass colors the nave with golden light. The tombs of Dom Pedro II, Empress Teresa Cristina, and Princess Isabel rest in the side chapel.

Palácio de Cristal is a glass-and-metal greenhouse prefabricated in France and assembled in Petrópolis in 1884. Princess Isabel organized exhibitions and high-society receptions here. Its transparent structure, with some panes tinted green and blue, now hosts exhibitions and concerts.

Avenida Koeler and Rua do Imperador concentrate the finest villas from the imperial era: neoclassical, neo-Gothic, Anglo-Norman, and Alpine chalets succeed each other along canals where the Quitandinha River flows. A Brazilian Paris lost in the mountains.

activities.text: Stroll through Petrópolis' historic center from the Imperial Museum to the Cathedral, passing Palácio Amarelo and the canals bordered by preserved Victorian villas.

Visit a craft brewery: Petrópolis, colonized by German immigrants in the 19th century, is Brazil's beer capital. The city's craft breweries produce lagers and ales following Germanic tradition, offering vat tours and tastings.
Sound and Light show at the Imperial Museum Thursday–Saturday evenings: the palace's pink facade lights up, projections evoke the imperial family's life and 19th-century Brazilian history.

map_keywords: Imperial Museum Palace Dom Pedro II Petrópolis

visits.text: The Chapadão dominates the coast from atop reddish cliffs eroded by wind and waves. This promontory offers panoramic views of the Atlantic, Praia do Amor below, and the rainforest carpeting the ravines.
Descend to Praia do Amor, whose bay forms a heart shape from the sky at low tide. Powerful waves make this Pipa's historic surf spot. Cliffs frame the beach like walls of a natural amphitheater.
Baía dos Golfinhos (Dolphin Bay) hides at the foot of vegetation-covered cliffs. Accessible at low tide by beach or by boat, it shelters a permanent dolphin colony that leaps just meters from shore.

Santuário Ecológico de Pipa protects a fragment of semi-arid Atlantic forest. Its 16 trails traverse cliffs, dunes, and viewpoints where you'll spot marmosets, exotic birds, and sometimes sea turtles during nesting season.

Praia do Madeiro unfolds its golden sand at the foot of cliffs draped in tropical forest. A sea turtle preservation zone, this beach has been ranked among the world's most beautiful by international travelers.

activities.text: Morning surf at Praia do Amor: consistent waves and Nordeste swell provide steady conditions. Rent a board on site and glide facing the cliffs—a setting that exists nowhere else.
Speedboat excursion to Baía dos Golfinhos and Guaraíras lagoon. Dolphins accompany the boat; the lagoon opens onto mangrove landscapes gilded by evening light.
Hike through the ecological sanctuary at sunset. Trails emerge onto vertiginous viewpoints above the ocean—with luck, a pod of dolphins plays in the waves below.
Evening stroll through Pipa's lively center. Cobbled streets overflow with seafood restaurants, cocktail bars, and live music—the cosmopolitan vibe of the Nordeste's most festive village.

days_plan.itin_title: Northeast Brazil Road Trip: Beaches and Dunes from Jericoacoara to Natal

visits.text: Pirenópolis, founded in 1727 by gold prospectors, is one of the oldest towns in Goiás. Its cobblestone streets, low colonial houses and three 18th-century churches form a historic center listed as a national heritage site. The Rio das Almas runs through town, its banks lined with restaurants and bars in restored buildings.

The Igreja de Nossa Senhora do Rosário, built in 1732, is the oldest church in Pirenópolis. Destroyed by fire in 2002, it was faithfully reconstructed and now houses the Museu da Matriz, which traces the restoration process and the town's sacred heritage.

The Museu das Cavalhadas displays the spectacular masks and embroidered costumes worn during the Cavalhadas de Pirenópolis, a horseback pageant reenacting the battle between Moors and Christians. Held every year at Pentecost since 1826, it is one of Brazil's grandest folk festivals.

The Cachoeira do Abade, 16 km from the center along a partly unpaved road, tumbles down a sandstone amphitheater deep in the cerrado forest. A hanging bridge and a panoramic trail let you approach the main cascade and its crystal-clear pools.

activities.text: Hike in the Parque Estadual dos Pireneus: the trail climbs to Pico dos Pireneus at 1,385 m, the highest point in the region. The view stretches across endless cerrado, reddish hills echoing the mining past and, on a clear day, the plains of Goiânia on the horizon.

Stroll along Rua do Lazer, the pedestrian street that comes alive in the evening. Restaurants serve galinhada goiana (chicken and rice with pequi), pamonha (corn paste steamed in its husk) and empada goiana, washed down with artisanal cachaça from the area.

Cool off at Cachoeira do Coqueiro or the Vargem Grande reserve, where the Santa Maria and Lázaro waterfalls tumble through regenerating cerrado. The cold natural pools offer welcome relief from the plateau heat.

days_plan.itin_title: Brazil Grand Tour: São Paulo, Iguaçu, the Northeast & Rio

visits.text: Early departure toward Poconé via BR-070 (102 km, 1h30 on paved road). This small colonial town, founded in 1781, was once the region's gold capital. Quick look at the Igreja Matriz de São Bento (1781), mandatory stop at the gas station—it's the last one for 147 km. If you have time, quick lunch at Maria do Cerrado to try vatapá pantaneiro. And off you go onto the Transpantaneira.

The red gate of the Transpantaneira marks the start of the adventure: 147 km of laterite dirt road to Porto Jofre, 122 wooden bridges (some being replaced with concrete since 2018). Built in 1976 to cross the Pantanal from end to end, it stopped abruptly at Porto Jofre—the ecosystem won against asphalt. The road has become a wildlife sanctuary: capybaras, caimans, jabirus, horned screamers, curassows. Drive at 30-40 km/h max—animal speed, not highway speed.

First stop at Pousada Piuval (km 10) or Pousada Pouso Alegre (km 33): these century-old fazendas have become ecolodges. You may not be sleeping here, but you can stop for lunch and a guided walk. Bullet ants with their ferocious sting, cathedral-sized termite mounds, tapir tracks in dried mud—the guide reads the landscape for you. Count on 2 hours on-site.

activities.text: The Transpantaneira's star activity: spotting hyacinth macaws (Anodorhynchus hyacinthinus), the world's largest parrot, 1-meter wingspan, cobalt blue with a yellow ring around the eye. Vulnerable species, protected—you'll observe them mainly around acuri palms whose nuts they crack with their beaks. Several fazendas (Aymara, Araras, San Francisco) shelter night roosts—be there around 4:30-5 pm to watch them come home.

At sunset, observe the jacarés (yacare caimans): over 10 million in the Pantanal, a world record. They line up by the dozens along the waterways, mouths open to regulate their temperature. Don't get out of the car without a guide. The Pantanal's evening soundscape—croaks, bird calls, distant howler monkey roars—beats any concert.

Dinner and overnight at a fazenda in the Pixaim stretch (around km 65): Hotel Pantanal Mato Grosso, Pousada Pouso Alegre, or Araras Eco Lodge. Pantanal cuisine, hammocks on verandas, starry sky without light pollution. Half-board included, expect R$600-1200 per night depending on comfort level. Night jeep safari available (sloths, armadillos, sometimes ocelots).

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Pantanal and Jaguar Capital: Cuiabá, Transpantaneira, and Porto Jofre in 6 Days

places.name: Ponte Alta Do Tocantins

visits.text: You arrive in Palmas, capital of Tocantins, and the road heads east. Three hours later, Ponte Alta do Tocantins marks the true gateway to Jalapão: this is where asphalt surrenders to red dirt tracks. The town itself is modest, but its position is strategic, 187 km from Palmas via the TO-030 then the TO-130. Upon arrival, head to Sussuapara Canyon, 15 km away: a narrow fissure between red walls where light barely filters through. Count on 25 minutes of downhill walking.

Sussuapara Canyon descends to 25 meters deep. At the bottom, trickles of water flow from overhanging roots and a small 5-meter jet falls into a natural pool where only one person fits at a time. The sandstone releases a reddish pigment on contact with water - you'll see it beneath your feet. The name comes from the sussuapara, a small deer from the region. Tip: descend around midday, it's the only time when sunlight truly penetrates to the bottom.

Built in 1989 when Tocantins State was carved out from Goiás, Ponte Alta lives up to its name: the town overlooks the Tocantins River. Take a walk through the center, grab a coffee at the rodoviária, watch the pickups equipped for the trail departing in convoy at dawn. It's also your last real opportunity to withdraw cash before heading toward Mateiros - ATMs become scarce inside the park.

Return west on the same sandy track. Pedra Furada is located about 30 km from Ponte Alta - not the same one as in Mateiros: here too the sandstone was hollowed into a portal by the wind, and late afternoon light filters through the hole like a natural projector. You get out of the 4x4, two minutes' walk through a eucalyptus grove, and you're there. Free visit.

In Pindorama do Tocantins, on the road, Lagoa do Japonês offers a refreshing stop: transparent waters with bluish hues, clear bottom, quieter atmosphere than the star sites. Many make it their last swim before returning to Palmas. Grass beach, a few tables under the trees.

On the final section, heading south before Ponte Alta, you cross capim dourado fields in full vereda - those fine golden stems you saw woven in Mumbuca. Ask your guide to stop for a few minutes on the roadside: backlit, in late afternoon, the vereda becomes literally golden. It's the image you'll keep of Jalapão beyond the dunes.

activities.text: Briefing with the agency for the coming days. Bag adjustments, 4x4 checks, buying water in quantity (count on 3 liters per person per day minimum), some snacks and dried fruits. Most accommodations inside the park operate without air conditioning and with limited electricity - pack your gear accordingly.

Taste Tocantins cuisine at dinner on the central praça: galinha caipira (slow-cooked free-range chicken), rice pilaf with pequi (a cerrado fruit with a powerful local flavor) and beiju, the tender manioc pancake. Restaurants close early, around 9 PM. It's also time to go to bed early - tomorrow, departure before 8 AM to tackle the 95 km dirt track to Mateiros.

Long 4x4 drive between Mumbuca and Ponte Alta - about 3 hours on track, sometimes more if the road condition has suffered from recent rains. Prepare a long playlist, keep water within reach and accept that half the day will be spent in the pickup. The landscape scrolls by: veredas of buritis, chapadões, sudden watercourses around a bend.

Evening in Ponte Alta: choose a simple churrasco on the praça, with grilled picanha and farofa. It's also an opportunity to buy a last kilo of dried capim dourado or a small hat if you didn't have time in Mumbuca.

places.name: Porto De Galinhas

visits.text: Porto de Galinhas—a former fishing village 70 kilometers south of Recife—owes its name to the era of slave trade: "There are new hens at the port" signaled the clandestine arrival of enslaved people. Today its low-rise streets are lined with seafood restaurants, craft shops, and galleries of painted rooster sculptures—a knowing nod to this dark history.

The natural pools of Porto de Galinhas form at low tide among coral reefs offshore. Traditional jangada sailboats ferry swimmers ten minutes to turquoise basins where tropical fish, starfish, and sea urchins drift through less than a meter of crystalline water. The "Mapa do Brasil"—a coral formation shaped like the country—is the iconic photo spot.

Pontal de Maracaípe, at the mouth of the Maracaípe River, shelters a mangrove ecosystem where seahorses reproduce. Jangada fishermen dive to show the tiny cavalos-marinhos in a jar before releasing them. Sunset from the pontal—with coconut palms and mangroves silhouetted against golden light—is the region's finest.

Projeto Hippocampus, in the village center, is a research facility dedicated to seahorse reproduction and conservation. Observation aquariums and educational displays explain the life cycle of these threatened creatures.

Praia de Muro Alto, to the north, boasts one of the region's largest natural pools: a coral wall traps water at low tide across more than a kilometer. Calm shallow water, white sand, and swaying palms—ideal for families.

activities.text: Walk barefoot along Porto de Galinhas beach at sunset. Multicolored jangadas are pulled up on the sand, beachside kiosks serve passion fruit caipirinhas, and the Atlantic stretches unbroken to Africa.

Buggy tour from beach to beach: Cupe for surfing, Maracaípe for waves and seahorses, Muro Alto for the expansive natural pools. Each stop reveals a different coastal landscape of reefs, dunes, and coconut groves.

Walk along the beach from Porto de Galinhas to Cupe, then onward to Muro Alto (8 kilometers one way). At low tide, Cupe's natural pools offer peaceful snorkeling away from the crowds near the village center.

places.name: Porto Jofre

visits.text: Early start for the second half of the Transpantaneira, wilder and more challenging: no electricity or phone signal, wooden bridges in worse condition. Count on 3 hours for 80 km. This section crosses the "Jaguar Ecological Reserve" (around km 100), one of the planet's highest jaguar densities. Drive very slowly—this is where you'll have the best encounters with tapirs, giant anteaters, marsh deer.

Arrival at Porto Jofre, a former fishing village at the end of the road, on the banks of the Rio Cuiabá. About ten buildings, two airstrips for air-taxi arrivals from Cuiabá (1-hour flight, alternative to 6 hours by road). Check into your lodge—Hotel Pantanal Norte is the largest and most comfortable (with pool), Pantanal Jaguar Camp and Dona Onça simpler but excellent for safaris.

Today, full-day boat safari: 6 to 8 hours on the Cuiabá, Piquiri, and Três Irmãos rivers. Departure around 6 am, return around 11 am for the heat break, then out again around 3 pm. Breakfast thermos served aboard. The Piquiri is narrower, wilder than the Cuiabá: better chances of witnessing a caiman hunt (Pantanal jaguars are the world's only big cats that regularly hunt caimans, taking prey up to 70 kg).

Lunch break on a white sandbank of the Rio Cuiabá. The lodge provides a cooler picnic: sandwiches, fruit, cupuaçu juice, water. Swimming not recommended (caimans, piranhas, occasional anaconda) except in areas supervised by the guide. Siesta in the shade, watching parrots come to drink at the water's edge.

Third and final day of boat safari. If you haven't yet had the "perfect jaguar" (seen up close, in good light, for a long time), today's your day. Statistically, over three days of safari in Porto Jofre during the dry season, travelers see an average of 4 to 7 different jaguars—each individual is identifiable by its rosettes, like fingerprints. The Panthera project maintains a catalog of over 200 jaguars tracked since 2010.

Morning variation: head up a small tributary in a silent electric-motor boat (some lodges have them). This is the photographers' option—less noise, less skittish animals, dawn light filtering through the vegetation. The price is higher (R$1500-2000 for a private half-day outing) but it's unbeatable for photography.

activities.text: First afternoon boat safari on the Rio Cuiabá. Four-hour outing (roughly 3-7 pm), flat-bottomed boat with 4-8 passengers and a tracker guide in VHF contact with other boats. When a jaguar is spotted, the info circulates in minutes. The cats rest on the riverbanks between 3 and 6 pm, sometimes 5 meters from the boat, completely indifferent. The probability of seeing a jaguar is 80-90% in July-August-September, close to 99% over 3 days of safari.

At sunset, you head up the Rio Cuiabá toward the confluence with the Piquiri: this is the heart of Encontro das Águas State Park, created in 2004, 108,000 hectares. Hundreds of capybaras on the banks, bare-faced curassows, stork-billed kingfishers fishing, black caimans (jacaré-açu) motionless in shallow waters. The low light paints the sand orange. Return to the lodge after dark.

Observing giant river otters (ariranhas), endemic to South American rivers, up to 1.8 meters long, in family groups of 5-10 individuals, extremely vocal—their calls carry a kilometer. They fish in teams, chase juvenile caimans. Endangered species (3,000 individuals in the entire Pantanal). Easy photography—the group doesn't hide.

If luck is with you, it'll be the spectacular hunt: a jaguar crouched on the bank, diving into the river and grabbing a caiman. The sequence lasts 30 seconds, the fight is over in 2 minutes max. Rare but documented multiple times daily during high season in Porto Jofre. Otherwise, you might see a mother with cubs, or a dominant male marking his territory by rubbing a tree.

Free evening at the lodge: pool if at Hotel Pantanal Norte, reading under fans, chatting with other travelers (often photographers staying a week). If you're up for it, night canoe outing with spotlight: caimans, capybaras on land, sometimes a puma caught drinking (rare).

Birdwatching special in the afternoon: the Pantanal counts 656 bird species. Classic targets: tuiuiú (jabiru stork, the Pantanal's symbol bird, 1.4 meters tall, 2.2-meter wingspan), herons, egrets, cormorants, Amazon kingfisher, maguari stork, razor-billed curassow. On the banks, horned screamers (Anhima cornuta)—large black birds with a single horn. Log your sightings—it's a birder's dream terrain.

Farewell dinner around the Pantanal buffet: pacu, tambaqui ribs, saffron rice, farofa, fried bananas, doce de leite for dessert. Ask the tracker guide to share his best stories—jaguar fights, 6-meter anacondas, swimming tapirs caught mid-crossing. Lodge tradition: collective tip for the guide and pilots (10-15% of stay, in BRL or USD), given the evening before departure.

places.name: Porto Seguro

visits.text: The Centro Histórico of Porto Seguro, perched on a cliff, is the exact spot where the Portuguese landed in 1500. The Marco do Descobrimento, a stone column brought from Portugal, and the Igreja de Nossa Senhora da Penha (1535) overlook the ocean and the reefs.

Arraial d'Ajuda, across the river by ferry, is a bohemian village of colorful lanes, art galleries and colonial churches. Praia de Mucugê, backed by multicolored cliffs, is one of the most photographed beaches in Bahia.

Praia do Espelho, 25 km south of Arraial, ranks among the most beautiful beaches in Brazil. At low tide, natural pools between the reefs reflect the sky like a mirror — hence the name. White cliffs and turquoise water complete the picture.

Trancoso, a former Jesuit village founded in 1583, centers on the Quadrado, a rectangular grass square lined with colorful houses and the Igreja de São João Batista, one of the oldest churches in Brazil. The far end of the Quadrado opens onto a cliff overlooking the ocean.

activities.text: Swimming and snorkeling in the natural pools of Praia do Espelho or Coroa Vermelha. The coral reefs shelter tropical fish, starfish and seahorses.
Sunset from the Quadrado in Trancoso: the sky turns crimson above the cliff as bars set up tables on the grass. A passion-fruit caipirinha is non-negotiable.
