places.name: Recife

visits.text: Recife Antigo, the port district on the island of the same name, centers on the Praça do Marco Zero, starting point of the city founded by the Dutch. Rua do Bom Jesus is home to the Synagogue Kahal Zur Israel, the oldest in the Americas (1636), and restored warehouses turned galleries.

Olinda, 7 km from Recife, has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1982. Founded by the Portuguese in 1535, burned by the Dutch in 1631 and then rebuilt, it lines up twenty baroque churches, convents and pastel-colored colonial houses across eight hills facing the Atlantic.

The Convento de São Francisco in Olinda, founded in the 16th century, preserves 16 panels of Portuguese azulejo tiles depicting the life of Saint Francis. The cloister, gilded woodwork and painted sacristy are worth the visit alone.

The Alto da Sé, the highest point in Olinda, brings together the Catedral da Sé, tapioca stalls and a vertiginous viewpoint. From here, the view sweeps across tile rooftops, bell towers, a fringe of coconut palms and, in the distance, the skyscrapers of Recife.

activities.text: Walk the narrow streets of Olinda: artists' studios open their doors to galleries of naïve paintings, wood sculptures and carnival masks. Rua do Amparo has the best addresses.
Enjoy a stuffed tapioca at sunset from the Alto da Sé, paired with a cajá-fruit caipirinha. Street vendors set up their grills facing the panorama.
Swim at Boa Viagem, a 7 km urban beach lined with coconut palms and protected by a reef barrier. Swim only in signed areas.

visits.text: You leave Carolina for Riachão, about 100 km southeast, the chapada's second gateway. The road alternates between pavement and 25 to 30 km of dirt depending on the sites you're targeting. A gentler day after yesterday's 4x4 marathon. You overnight in Riachão to save time on the northeastern chapada attractions.

You discover Encanto Azul in early afternoon, a small lake formed by a spring gushing between rock walls. The color—saturated turquoise blue—comes from the white sand bottom and light filtering through the vegetation. No infrastructure on site except restrooms; entrance is very cheap. Arrive early—by 9 a.m. the site fills up and the perfect photo requires perfectly still water.

Six kilometers from Encanto Azul, you continue to Poço Azul, part of Reserva Natural Cachoeira do Rio Cocal. A private property crossed by the Cocal River, which forms four waterfalls in total. The poço itself is a deep blue pool where you can swim, depending on river conditions. Good infrastructure: buffet restaurant, changing rooms, rest area. During heavy rains, swimming may be prohibited for safety reasons.

activities.text: If you're ready to push on to Cachoeira Santa Bárbara, expect an additional walk on a suspension footbridge of about 700 meters. The falls stand 76 meters high, the chapada's tallest. Depending on season and conditions, swimming at the base may or may not be possible—call ahead to check.
For thrill-seekers, Poço Azul offers rappelling down Cachoeira Santa Bárbara—70 meters to the natural pool. Supervised activity, book in the morning on site subject to availability.

days_plan.itin_title: Chapada das Mesas - 6 Days of Canyons and Waterfalls in Southern Maranhão

places.name: Rio De Janeiro Leme

visits.text: Corcovado peaks at 710 m. Christ the Redeemer, unveiled in 1931, stands 28 m tall with arms outstretched. From the summit platform: Copacabana, Ipanema, Guanabara Bay, Sugarloaf and Tijuca Forest spread out in a full 360° sweep.
Sugarloaf (396 m) is reached by two cable cars from Praia Vermelha. The view takes in the entire city, and is especially stunning in the late afternoon when golden light floods the bay.
The Escadaria Selarón is a stairway of 215 steps covered with over 2,000 ceramic tiles collected from around the world. It links the Lapa neighborhood to Santa Teresa.
Santa Teresa is Rio's Montmartre: steep cobblestone lanes, colorful colonial houses and artists' studios.

Tijuca Forest covers 33 km² — the largest urban tropical forest in the world. Ocelots, monkeys and 300 bird species. Parque Lage at its entrance combines gardens, waterfalls, a 19th-century mansion and a contemporary art gallery.
The Cathedral of São Sebastião, built between 1964 and 1979, is a 96-meter-tall concrete cone 104 meters in diameter. Four giant stained-glass panels run from floor to ceiling.

The Tijuca National Park shelters 33 km² of dense tropical rainforest within the city limits — the world's largest urban jungle. Trails lead to the Mesa do Imperador lookout and the Cascatinha Taunay waterfall, a 30-meter cascade tucked into the canopy. At the park's entrance, Parque Lage pairs a stately 19th-century mansion with lush gardens and a contemporary art school.

São Sebastião Cathedral rises in the city center like a massive concrete cone, 96 meters tall and 104 meters across. Inside, four floor-to-ceiling stained-glass panels pour colored light into the cavernous nave, creating an almost otherworldly atmosphere.

The Museu do Amanhã (Museum of Tomorrow), opened in 2015 on the revamped Praça Mauá waterfront, is an architectural feat by Santiago Calatrava. Its interactive exhibitions explore humanity's major environmental and climate challenges.

Praça XV and its surroundings form the colonial heart of Rio. The Paço Imperial (1743), the former royal residence, the Igreja Nossa Senhora do Carmo and the lanes of the old Portuguese town tell the story of Rio before the beaches.

Rio's Jardim Botânico, founded in 1808 by Dom João VI, spreads across 54 hectares at the foot of Corcovado. Its imperial avenue of royal palms, planted in 1842, is one of the most famous in Brazil. Orchids, bromeliads, Victoria Amazonica water lilies and a butterfly greenhouse.

Ipanema and Leblon, Rio's two chicest beaches, stretch 4 km along the Atlantic. Numbered kiosks (postos) structure beach life: each posto has its own vibe, its crowd, and its vendors selling mate and açaí.

The Museu Nacional de Belas Artes, founded in 1937 in a neoclassical palace downtown, holds the largest collection of 19th-century Brazilian painting as well as European works brought over by the royal court in 1808.

activities.text: Early-morning walk along the Copacabana mosaic promenade (4.5 km) to Ipanema: jogging, beach volleyball, surfing. Leave valuables at the hotel.
Hike from Parque Lage to Corcovado: 3 hours through Tijuca's tropical jungle, +700 m elevation gain.
Sunset cruise on Guanabara Bay: Sugarloaf and Corcovado seen from the water.
Head to Arpoador, between Copacabana and Ipanema, 30 minutes before sunset. The cariocas applaud as the sun disappears.
Early-morning walk along the Copacabana mosaic promenade (4.5 km) to Ipanema: jogging, beach volleyball, surfing. Leave valuables at the hotel.
Head to Arpoador, between Copacabana and Ipanema, 30 minutes before sunset. The cariocas applaud as the sun disappears.
Visit the Niterói Museum of Contemporary Art (MAC) across the bay: Oscar Niemeyer's white flying-saucer perched on a cliff, with a panoramic view back to Rio. Reach it by ferry from downtown.
Saturday feijoada lunch in Santa Teresa or the Lapa district: a slow-cooked stew of black beans and smoked meats, caipirinhas and live samba. The quintessential carioca weekend ritual.
Ipanema Hippie Fair (Praça General Osório) on Sunday morning: crafts, jewelry, paintings and semi-precious stones. The largest street market in Rio.

Hike Dois Irmãos from the Vidigal favela: 45 minutes up to the twin peaks that tower over Ipanema. A 360° view of the coastline, the lagoons, Christ the Redeemer and the bay. The trailhead is at the top of Vidigal, reached by communal moto-taxi.

visits.text: Arrive in Salvador de Bahia via Luís Eduardo Magalhães International Airport. Transfer to Pelourinho, 30 km, 45 min. Drop your bags at a pousada in the historic center. The afternoon is enough for your first immersion into Brazil's Afro-Brazilian capital.

Pelourinho, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1985, was the central square of Brazil's first capital (1549-1763) from the 16th to 18th centuries. Its name recalls the whipping post where slaves were punished. The restored pastel facades, Igreja Nossa Senhora do Rosário dos Pretos and Praça Terreiro de Jesus form South America's most cohesive colonial ensemble.

The Igreja e Convento de São Francisco ranks among the world's baroque masterpieces. Austere exterior facade, interior entirely covered with 800 kilos of gold leaf on carved wood, Portuguese azulejo tiles lining the cloister walls. Built between 1708 and 1723 by Franciscan friars. Admission 10 BRL.

Take the Elevador Lacerda, an Art Deco elevator from 1873 that connects the upper city to the lower city in 30 seconds (72 m drop). Obligatory postcard shot. Fare 1 BRL since the February 2025 reopening (free with SalvadorCard). Once down, head to Mercado Modelo for handicrafts (capoeira, berimbaus, hammocks) and views over Baía de Todos os Santos.

Free morning in Lençóis. Options depending on your flight time: easy walk to Poço Halley (20 min from center, quiet swimming), visit the Museu do Garimpo we didn't have time for on D2, handicraft shopping (semi-precious stone jewelry, banana and umbu sweets).

More active option: half-day excursion to Marimbus, wetland nicknamed the Chapada's "mini-Pantanal." Canoe crossing on Rio Roncador amid buritis (marsh palms), water lilies, jacaré alligators and aquatic birds. Count on 150 BRL per person, 4h all-inclusive.

Transfer to Horácio de Matos Airport, 20 km, 30 min. Azul flight Lençóis-Salvador (1h) mid-afternoon. Three weekly frequencies: absolutely verify the day before booking. Alternative if no flight: Real Expresso bus Lençóis-Salvador (6h, 120 BRL).

Arrive in Salvador in the evening. Depending on international return flight time, extra night downtown or direct transfer to Luís Eduardo Magalhães Airport. Flights to Europe typically depart between 10 PM and 11 PM (via GRU or direct).

activities.text: Dine in Pelourinho at a Bahian restaurant. Signature dishes: moqueca de peixe (fish in coconut milk and dendê oil), acarajé (black-eyed pea fritters fried in palm oil), vatapá. Uauá or Axego on Rua João de Deus are local favorites that avoid the tourist traps.

On Tuesday evenings, catch free capoeira and Afro-percussion shows on Praça Pedro Archanjo. Groups like Olodum and Filhos de Gandhy perform regularly. Unique atmosphere, but keep an eye on your belongings. Avoid isolated alleys at night.

If staying overnight in Salvador, final Bahian dinner in the chic Rio Vermelho neighborhood (Boteco do França, Paraíso Tropical). Traditional moqueca, authentic dendê, passion fruit caipirinha. Last moment of Bahian spirit before returning home.

Bring back artisanal Chapada cachaça: Seleta or Bocoy brands, produced in Vale do Paraguaçu. On sale at Lençóis Municipal Market or the airport. Count on 60-100 BRL per artisanal bottle. Checked baggage required for international flights.

places.name: Sao Jorge Vila

visits.text: Leave Alto Paraíso for São Jorge, the small gateway village to the national park, 36 kilometers away on GO-239. The streets are red dirt, the pousadas rustic, the vibe hippie-eco. Drop your things and walk five minutes to the visitor center: this is where you buy your park ticket (47 R$ standard rate, book online at Parquetur's site), watch the mandatory orientation video, and sign the waiver. The park opens 8 AM to 6 PM, last entry around noon.

Trilha dos Saltos is the park's grand classic. Roughly 10 to 11 kilometers round trip for a full day out. The viewpoint for Salto 120 comes after 4 kilometers of walking: the waterfall drops in a vertical ribbon 120 meters into a canyon you'll only see from above, the pool being inaccessible. Continue 800 meters to Salto 80, whose wide, deep pool allows swimming. The water is ice-cold, invigorating after the hike. Follow the yellow arrows all the way.

Before heading back, push on to the Corredeiras do Rio Preto. A flat 1.5-kilometer trail from Salto 80 leads to a series of small cascading pools—natural hydromassage where the water runs less cold than elsewhere. A boardwalk accessible to people with reduced mobility was built in 2019. Park guides advise skipping the 12–2 PM portion on the return: with no shade, the sun beats down hard.

Second entry into the park, this time via the trilha vermelha—red arrows. Head for Cânions I and II, two gorges carved by the Rio Preto where water flows between quartzite walls. Cânion II offers a large swimming pool, deep and ice-cold, perfect for a plunge after the approach walk (4 kilometers from the entrance). Cânion I is a bit higher up the trail, with choppier water. Both are on the same loop.

Virtually on the same loop, Cachoeira das Cariocas strings together several small cascades and stepped pools. The trail leading there extends the canyon route; count on 30 minutes of additional walking. This is where you'll encounter the fewest people in the park: groups usually stay at Cânion II. Pack a snack—the spot invites lingering.

On the return, make a detour via Trilha do Cerrado (Seriema), a short, flat 800-meter path through plants typical of the biome: ipê, lobeira, paepalanthus. A local guide will explain, if you've hired one, why the cerrado is one of the world's oldest and most diverse tropical ecosystems, with 350 to 400 vascular plant species per hectare. It's also a good spot for birdwatching late afternoon.

activities.text: Evening in São Jorge slows to a complete halt. Go for dinner at Risoteria Santo Cerrado, which serves risottos made with cerrado ingredients (pequi, baru). The village has only about ten restaurants, all within walking distance of each other. No nightclub, no shopping center—just campfires at certain pousadas and an exceptional starry sky thanks to zero light pollution.

The park offers visitation noturna Wednesday through Sunday evenings: groups of six maximum, PNCV-accredited guide, headlamps mandatory (phones are not allowed). The sky here, far from cities, reveals the Milky Way to the naked eye. Book in advance at the visitor center—the ticket must be purchased before the ticket office closes.

To break the rhythm, settle in at Mansa e Brava, an accessible stretch of the Rio São Miguel 10 minutes by car from São Jorge. Pebble beaches, shallow water on the "Mansa" side, playful currents on the "Brava" side. Brazilian families spend the afternoon here with coolers and ice-cold Coke. No entry fee. Ideal for resting your legs after two days of trilhas.

In the evening, ask your pousada to arrange an appointment with a local therapist for a bamboo massage or herbal bath with cerrado plants. Healing practices have been an integral part of Alto Paraíso and São Jorge's identity since the 1990s, inherited from the alternative communities that settled on the fourteenth parallel.

places.name: Sao Luis

visits.text: São Luís's Centro Histórico preserves over 3,500 buildings from the 17th, 18th, and 19th centuries—the largest collection of Portuguese colonial architecture in Latin America. More than 300 different azulejo tile patterns dress the facades, imported from Portugal starting in the 1840s to protect walls from tropical humidity. The district has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1997.

Walk up Rua Portugal, where you'll find the city's highest concentration of azulejo-clad facades. Stone pavement leads to Beco Catarina Mina, a narrow alley flanked by sobrados with wrought-iron balconies. A museum dedicated to azulejo tiles occupies the ground floor of a restored townhouse.

The Palácio dos Leões, seat of the Maranhão state government, has overlooked the Bay of São Marcos since the 17th century. Behind its neoclassical facade lie reception rooms furnished with period pieces and paintings. The neighboring Igreja da Sé cathedral preserves gilded altars and a restored pipe organ.

The Teatro Arthur Azevedo, inaugurated in the 19th century, is one of Brazil's oldest theaters still in operation. Its Italian-style hall seats 800 beneath a painted ceiling. Guided tours reveal the original stage machinery still in working condition.

activities.text: Stroll across the Praça Grande esplanade at sunset: the pastel facades turn golden while viewpoints offer sweeping vistas over the Bay of São Marcos and the historic center's terracotta rooftops.

São Luís is Brazil's reggae capital. Come evening, bars in the historic quarter pulse with reggae danced in pairs—a unique local tradition. Bumba-meu-boi, a folkloric spectacle mixing dance, music, and embroidered costumes, brings the squares to life during festival season.

Sample Maranhão cuisine in the alleyways of Reviver: cuxá (a sauce made with local herbs served over rice), grilled fish with tucupi, and juçara juice—the Amazonian cousin of açaí—sold by street vendors.

places.name: Sao Miguel Do Gostoso

visits.text: São Miguel do Gostoso occupies Ponta do Santo Cristo, one of South America's easternmost points. Trade winds blow here with rare consistency—the region is among the windiest places on the planet.
Praia de Tourinhos, semi-deserted, harbors a petrified dune 8 meters high—a unique geological formation sculpted by wind over millennia. Reefs form natural pools at low tide.
Four urban beaches string along the village: Ponta do Santo Cristo for kitesurfing, Cardeiro for natural pools, Xepa for atmosphere, and Maceió for fresh seafood facing lush vegetation.

Igreja São Miguel Arcanjo, in the village center, bears the patron saint's name that gave the town its identity. Around it, dirt streets preserve the atmosphere of an authentic Nordeste fishing village.

activities.text: Kitesurfing or windsurfing session at Ponta do Santo Cristo. Winds of 20-25 knots blow nearly every day in season—world-class conditions with 29°C water.
Sunset at Praia de Tourinhos: grazing light sets the petrified dune and brown cliffs ablaze. Dried fish and ice-cold beer on the beach—Gostoso's evening ritual.
Buggy ride along the coast to Praia do Marco, where the first colonial marker was planted in Brazil in 1501. Thirty kilometers of virgin beaches, dunes, and coconut palms roll beneath the wheels.

places.name: Sao Paulo

visits.text: MASP (São Paulo Museum of Art) hovers above Avenida Paulista on four red pillars. Lina Bo Bardi designed it in 1968 so the space beneath would remain open to the public. The collection spans Van Gogh, Rembrandt, Picasso, Monet, and Brazilian masters.

Ibirapuera Park, designed by Oscar Niemeyer and Roberto Burle Marx for São Paulo's 400th anniversary in 1954, sprawls across 1.6 km². Lakes, cycling paths, the Ibirapuera Auditorium, Afro Brasil Museum, and Museum of Modern Art share this sprawling green lung.

Mercado Municipal (Mercadão), a covered market hall from 1933, shelters remarkable stained-glass mosaics. Spices, tropical fruits, smoked meats, cheeses, and the legendary mortadella sandwich—São Paulo's gastronomic icon.
Liberdade, São Paulo's Japanese quarter, is home to the largest Japanese community outside Japan. Red lanterns, specialty groceries, authentic Japanese restaurants, and a Sunday street market.

Pinacoteca do Estado, founded in 1905, is São Paulo's oldest art museum. Its neoclassical building renovated by Paulo Mendes da Rocha holds Brazil's largest collection of 19th and 20th-century Brazilian art: paintings, sculptures, and works by Paulista modernists.

Praça da Sé is Brazil's kilometer zero—all distances in the country radiate from here. The neo-Gothic cathedral inaugurated in 1954, with its twin 92-meter towers, dominates the largest square in the historic center.

Museu de Arte Sacra de São Paulo occupies an 18th-century Benedictine convent. Its collection of 18,000 pieces—ex-votos, baroque sculptures, colonial silverwork—is Latin America's largest in sacred art.

Bairro da Bixiga (Bela Vista) is São Paulo's Italian-Brazilian quarter. Cobblestone streets, century-old cantinas, and independent theaters tell the story of 19th-century Italian immigration. On Sunday mornings, the market at Praça Dom Orione blends secondhand finds and street food.

activities.text: On Sundays, Avenida Paulista closes to traffic. Under MASP, an antiques market springs up; across the street, Bixiga's artisan market offers street food and live music.
Street art walk through Vila Madalena: Beco do Batman concentrates spectacular murals. Independent galleries, designer boutiques, and trendy bars fill this bohemian neighborhood.
Dinner at a neighborhood churrascaria: all-you-can-eat grilled meats rodízio-style, caipirinhas, and Brazilian music. Skip the touristy downtown spots; head for Jardins or Itaim instead.
Climb to Terraço Itália (41st floor of Edifício Itália) to watch São Paulo unfurl endlessly. The world's fourth-largest metropolis reveals its dizzying scale at sunset.

Walking tour of the historic center: Edifício Martinelli (Brazil's first skyscraper, 1929), Oscar Niemeyer's Edifício Copan, Viaduto do Chá and its views over the old downtown. Architecture that charts the city's waves of modernization.

Visit the Museu do Futebol beneath the stands of Pacaembu stadium: Brazilian football history told through sound archives, legendary jerseys, and immersive installations. Pelé, Garrincha, and World Cup glory on display.
Evening in Pinheiros or Jardins: designer cocktail bars, celebrated chef restaurants, intense cultural and music scene. São Paulo is one of the world's gastronomic capitals.

places.name: Sao Paulo Paulista

visits.text: The Mercado Municipal (Mercadão), a covered market hall from 1933, features stunning stained-glass mosaics. Browse spices, tropical fruits, smoked meats, artisan cheeses, and the legendary mortadella sandwich—a gastronomic emblem of the city that locals swear by.

MASP (São Paulo Museum of Art) floats above Avenida Paulista on four iconic red pillars. Lina Bo Bardi designed it in 1968 so the space beneath would remain open to the public. Inside: Van Gogh, Rembrandt, Picasso, Monet, and an exceptional Brazilian art collection.

Ibirapuera Park, designed by Oscar Niemeyer and Roberto Burle Marx for São Paulo's 400th anniversary in 1954, sprawls across 1.6 square kilometers. Lakes, cycling paths, the Ibirapuera Auditorium, Afro Brasil Museum, and the Museum of Modern Art await.

Liberdade, São Paulo's Japanese quarter, is home to the largest Japanese community outside Japan. Red lanterns overhead, specialist groceries, authentic Japanese restaurants, and a vibrant Sunday street market.

activities.text: Dinner at a neighborhood churrascaria: all-you-can-eat rodízio of grilled meats, caipirinhas flowing, and Brazilian music filling the air. Head to Jardins or Itaim for the best spots.
Street art walk through Vila Madalena: Beco do Batman concentrates spectacular murals in a single alley. Independent galleries, designer boutiques, and hip bars line the surrounding streets.
Climb to Terraço Itália (41st floor of Edifício Itália) to watch São Paulo unfold infinitely beneath you at sunset.

places.name: Sao Raimundo Nonato

visits.text: São Raimundo Nonato is the mandatory base for exploring the park. This town of roughly 35,000 inhabitants concentrates everything you won't find elsewhere: hotels, banks, supermarkets, restaurants. Since 2025, NSR airport no longer has regular commercial flights—access is via Petrolina (PE), 303 km away, about 4.5 hours along BR-235 then BR-324, now entirely paved. Drop your bags, locate the agency providing your guide, and save the afternoon for the Museu do Homem Americano, which lays the groundwork for everything you'll see next.

The Museu do Homem Americano (FUMDHAM), opened in 1998 in São Raimundo Nonato, displays artifacts from over forty years of excavations: lithic tools, pottery fragments, human remains. This is where the skull from Toca dos Coqueiros is exhibited—it shows distinct characteristics from classic Amerindians and fuels the debate over early American settlement. Allow two hours to cover the entire collection. The foundation, created by Niède Guidon in 1986, manages the park in partnership with ICMBio and IPHAN.

Make use of late afternoon with a visit to the Fábrica de Cerâmica Serra da Capivara in Coronel José Dias, 30 km from São Raimundo. The workshop was founded in 1995 by Niède Guidon as both a social and artistic project: it reproduces the park's rock art motifs on ceramics and provides employment for several dozen local artisans. It's also the only place to buy serious reproductions, made on-site—far superior to market copies. Open Monday through Saturday, reasonable prices.

The Desfiladeiro da Capivara, roughly 40 km from São Raimundo, was the first circuit Niède Guidon explored in the 1970s and remains one of the most visited. First stop: Toca da Entrada do Pajaú, a small rock shelter whose paintings date to around 12,000 years BP. You'll see hunting scenes, dances, daily life, painted in red from iron oxides. The circuit is of medium difficulty—stone paths, stairs, moderate elevation changes.

Toca do Inferno sits in a deep canyon, lacking paintings but boasting a rock cathedral with walls over 50 meters high in sandstone. Local legend claims a mysterious chant rises from the depths at noon—guides enjoy putting you to the test. Further along, Toca da Entrada do Baixão da Vaca offers 749 rock paintings spread across 100 meters of wall, one of the densest panels in the park. Watch out for snakes and bees in the shaded recesses.

To finish the circuit, Toca do Paraguaio was the first site officially catalogued by researchers. Two skeletons were recovered here, dated respectively to 7,000 and 8,670 years BP. The paintings, from the Nordeste tradition, sometimes exceed 1.5 meters in height—rare in the park. Tip: get a guide who'll point out the details (running hunter, copulation scene, animal figures) because some panels are hard to read with the naked eye.

Serra Branca is the latest major circuit opened in the park, about 50 km from São Raimundo. Low difficulty, a nice day to rest your legs after yesterday. The olho d'água da Serra Branca, a natural spring, was the only water source for inhabitants before the park's creation in 1979. Several families were relocated to the povoado Novo Zabelê in compensation. The circuit includes sites Toca do Vento, Toca Extrema II, Toca do Veado, and Toca do João Arsena.

Along this circuit, you'll also find remnants of the 'maniçobeiros,' families who extracted latex from the maniçoba (Manihot glaziovii) in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, during the rubber boom period. The local rock paintings, later than those at Desfiladeiro, depict hunting, warfare, and sexuality scenes with remarkable narrative precision. The stylistic tradition called 'Nordeste' has been studied since the 1980s by Anne-Marie Pessis, Guidon's longtime collaborator.

Sítio João Pimenta, 20 km from São Raimundo in the Serra Vermelha region, is a private reserve (RPPN) managed by the descendants of João Pimenta, a former traditional occupant of the area. The trail is 1.5 km round-trip with sandstone formations that have been compared to Turkey's Cappadocia—exaggerated, but the atmosphere is superb. A few rock art sites and, by reservation, rappelling is possible. Visits in small groups only.

The Trilha Hombu—the word means 'come see, come with me' in a local indigenous language according to guides—is one of the most comprehensive circuits to wrap up your visit. It blends prehistoric sites (Toca da Invenção, Toca da Pedra Caída, Tocas da Ema do Sítio do Brás) with recent history: the Casa do Neco Coelho, one of the first families settled in the area before the park's creation, and the small Museu do Neco Coelho which traces the lives of 20th-century residents.

Along this circuit, the guide is often a descendant of the region's historic families—sometimes a son or grandson of the former mateiro guides who accompanied Niède Guidon from the earliest expeditions. The narration thus weaves together scientific archaeology and family memory, lending the route a special character. The circuit includes an option to climb, via a ladder planted in the rock, to the top of the chapadão for a panoramic view.

Before heading back to Petrolina, stop by downtown São Raimundo Nonato for last-minute shopping at the Fábrica de Cerâmica if you didn't make it on D1, or to buy copies of rock paintings directly from the artisans. The town lives largely off the park and the jobs it generates—every purchase counts.

activities.text: Briefing with your guide (mandatory for park entry) on the next four days' program: choosing circuits according to ability level, packing your bag, water (3 liters minimum per person), closed-toe shoes, hat, flashlight for dark shelters. Ask your guide how to book evening access to Boqueirão da Pedra Furada when the site is lit up.

Dinner on the central praça: carne de sol (dried and grilled meat), baião de dois (rice and beans melded together), grilled queijo coalho, all washed down with cajá or umbu juice—caatinga fruits you won't find anywhere else. Restaurants close around 10 p.m.

Lunch in the povoado of Barreirinho, near the park: local restaurant, comida caseira, galinha caipira, paçoca, mandacaru salad. Many agencies include this in the package. It's also a good time to chat with your guide about the park's oral history—many locals saw Niède Guidon arrive in the 1970s, and their families contributed to the excavations.

On the way back, detour through Baixão das Andorinhas in late afternoon to watch the swallows return to the canyon. The spectacle lasts about 30 minutes: thousands of birds pour into the cliff at dusk to roost. Viewing platform provided, easy car access.

Visit the povoado of Novo Zabelê, created to resettle families displaced when the park was established in 1979. About a hundred inhabitants now live from beekeeping, cerrado fruit preserves, honey, and handicrafts. The project is partly supported by FUMDHAM. It's a chance to talk with the elders, who still remember the caves their parents frequented long before they became archaeological sites.

For the more adventurous, the Caldeirão dos Rodrigues circuit offers a scalaminhada (half-climbing, half-hiking) on the plateau, passing through Toca do Caldeirão dos Rodrigues—one of the park's finest rock art compositions, dated to around 18,000 years BP according to excavations. Panoramic view of Grotão da Esperança canyon from the summit. Difficult level, plan 4 to 5 hours, bring plenty of water.

Depending on your flight, drive to Petrolina in the afternoon: 303 km, about 4.5 hours on BR-324 then BR-235. The road is now entirely paved, passes through Dirceu Arcoverde, Remanso (BA), Casa Nova, and runs alongside the Rio São Francisco toward the end. Plan to leave no later than 2 p.m. for an evening flight from Petrolina.

If you arrive in Petrolina early, detour to the banks of the Rio São Francisco at sunset. The city is twinned with Juazeiro (BA) on the opposite bank and this is one of South America's greatest rivers. A few floating restaurants serve grilled surubim, the river's king fish.

places.name: Teresina

visits.text: The Ponte Estaiada, a cable-stayed bridge illuminated at night, spans the Rio Poti above Parque Potycabana. Its pedestrian walkway offers views of both banks of Teresina — the only state capital in the Northeast that is not on the coast.

The Parque Encontro dos Rios, at the confluence of the Rio Poti and the Rio Parnaíba, forms a natural promontory from which you can watch the two rivers meet — one light, one dark. Herons, kingfishers and iguanas inhabit the banks.

Praça Pedro II, the historic heart of Teresina founded in 1852, is flanked by the Palácio Karnak (seat of state government) and the Theatro 4 de Setembro, one of the oldest theaters in the Northeast. Balconied houses along Rua Álvaro Mendes recall the imperial era.

The Centro de Artesanato Mestre Dezinho gathers the work of Piauí's artisans under one roof: Piauí opals, buriti-wood sculptures, painted ceramics and bilro bobbin lace inherited from the Portuguese tradition.

activities.text: Browse the Mercado Central of Teresina: cajuína (fermented cashew drink), buriti sweets, coalho cheese and crafts made from carnaúba palm straw — the emblematic palm of Piauí.

places.name: Teresopolis

visits.text: Serra dos Órgãos National Park, created in 1939, spans 20,000 hectares between Teresópolis and Petrópolis. Its name comes from its granite peaks that evoke a cathedral's organ pipes. Dedo de Deus—the Finger of God—rises to 1,692 m and appears on Rio de Janeiro State's flag.
Mirante do Soberbo is the park's iconic viewpoint: a panoramic terrace accessible by car from Teresópolis headquarters, with a direct view of Dedo de Deus and the entire Serra dos Órgãos range.

The park's lower section from the Teresópolis entrance offers manicured gardens, accessible waterfalls, natural swimming pools, and the Trilha Suspensa (1.3 km elevated boardwalk trail, wheelchair-accessible).

Igreja Santa Teresa d'Ávila, a neo-Gothic church overlooking Teresópolis' central square, gives the city its name—named after Teresa Léoni who acquired it in the 19th century. It marks the starting point for any downtown visit.

activities.text: Trilha Cartão Postal hike (1.2 km) from park entrance: climb to viewpoint at 1,320 m elevation, spectacular view of Dedo de Deus and the entire range. Easy, round-trip in 2 hours.
Trilha Pedra do Sino hike for seasoned hikers: 8h round-trip, passing Véu da Noiva waterfall. From the summit at 2,263 m, Guanabara Bay and Rio de Janeiro spread across the horizon.
Climbing on the park's granite walls: Dedo de Deus is considered the birthplace of sport climbing in Brazil. Local operators offer guided outings for all levels.

places.name: Tiradentes

visits.text: Drive from Ouro Preto to Congonhas via BR-040 (85 km, 1h50). Congonhas is a fairly ordinary modern mining town, but it shelters one of the twin summits of world baroque: the Santuário Bom Jesus de Matosinhos. Park at the sanctuary lot.

The Santuário Bom Jesus de Matosinhos, UNESCO-listed in 1985, dominates the city from atop Monte Maranhão. Founded in 1757 by Portuguese settler Feliciano Mendes as payment for a healing vow, it brings together a basilica and six Passion chapels. You climb a monumental ramp flanked by six chapels and then encounter the Prophets.

The twelve Prophets, carved in soapstone between 1800 and 1805 by an Aleijadinho already ravaged by disease, form the absolute pinnacle of his work. Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, Daniel, and eight others answer each other from a distance in theatrical poses, hands raised, scrolls unfurled—designed to be "read" from the bottom of the forecourt.

In the six Passion chapels, 66 life-size polychrome cedar sculptures, created by Aleijadinho and his apprentices between 1796 and 1799, depict Christ's Passion. The polychromy, executed by Mestre Ataíde, gives each face a striking intensity. One of the world's most complete sacred sculptural ensembles.

Take the road to Tiradentes, 170 km southwest via BR-265 (2h45). Arrive late afternoon. Leave the car at the town entrance parking: the center is strictly pedestrian. Drop your bags at the pousada and set out to observe the village in the low-angled light.

Morning in Tiradentes. Begin at the Igreja Matriz de Santo Antônio on the heights. Built from 1710, it's considered one of mineiro baroque's masterpieces. The design (risco) of its rococo facade was commissioned from Aleijadinho in 1810, but execution was carried out by other craftsmen after his death in 1814. The interior is one of colonial Brazil's most richly gilded, with altarpieces and pulpits covered in gold leaf.

The Portuguese organ preserved inside, installed in the late 18th century and still functional, is one of Brazil's rare baroque instruments. Regular concerts (Friday, Sunday). The church also preserves ceiling paintings attributed to Antônio de Caldas, rare representations of baroque allegorical scenes.

Descend to the Chafariz de São José, built in 1749. It supplied the town with drinking water via a one-kilometer aqueduct built by enslaved laborers. Three distinct basins: washerwomen, horse trough, running water for the population. The ensemble still functions today.

Allow about 1h to drive Tiradentes → São João del-Rei (12 km) and board the Maria Fumaça for the return journey to Tiradentes. Departures generally leave São João del-Rei at 10am and 1:30pm, returns from Tiradentes at 11am and 2:30pm (Friday-Sunday). The 762mm narrow-gauge line, inaugurated in 1881 in the presence of Dom Pedro II, is Brazil's oldest Maria Fumaça still in operation. Pulled by period steam locomotives. 12 km journey in 35-40 min.

São João del-Rei, population 90,000, is the commercial and university town complementing Tiradentes. Visit the Catedral Basílica Nossa Senhora do Pilar (1721) on the main square. The church preserves a late 18th-century baroque organ and two bells cast in Portugal that give the city its nickname "terra onde os sinos falam" (land where the bells speak).

The Igreja São Francisco de Assis (1774), near the humpback bridge, has a unique curvilinear facade attributed to Aleijadinho's school. Inside rests Tancredo Neves, president-elect in 1985 who died before his inauguration. The neighboring Memorial Tancredo Neves chronicles Brazil's 1985 democratic transition.

activities.text: Lunch in Congonhas before departing. The town has no great gastronomic tradition, but Restaurante da Praça facing the sanctuary serves a decent mineiro buffet at 45 BRL. Quick and efficient.

Dinner at Largo das Forras, Tiradentes' central square and nightlife hub. Contemporary mineira cuisine restaurants (Tragaluz, Theobroma, Uai d'Italia). Book ahead on weekends: Tiradentes is one of Brazil's major gastronomic destinations.

Lunch in São João del-Rei. Restaurante Churrascaria Ramon serves a solid meat rodízio at a gentle price (70 BRL). For a more typical option, Quinto do Ouro offers traditional mineira cuisine in a vaulted room.

Return to Tiradentes late afternoon. If time permits, detour via the Serra de São José, the limestone massif overlooking the town. Short trails (1-3h) to Cachoeira do Mangue and viewpoints. Guide recommended (50 BRL): trails are poorly marked.

Evening in Tiradentes. Sunset aperitif on a terrace at Largo das Forras, then gastronomic dinner. Tragaluz (contemporary mineira cuisine) and Pousada Thymus (artisanal cachaça cellar) are two benchmarks. Calm, romantic town in the evening.

visits.text: From Caburé, the route follows the beach to Tutóia, the Maranhão gateway to the Parnaíba Delta. This is the only delta in the Americas that opens directly into the open sea: the Parnaíba River splits into five channels forming over 80 islands across 2,700 km².

Navigate the labyrinth of the Delta: narrow igarapés (channels) wind through mangroves whose aerial roots plunge into brackish water. Capuchin monkeys leap from branch to branch while iguanas bask on the banks.

Ilha das Canárias, the delta's largest island, hosts a fishing community and a traditional restaurant where you'll lunch on grilled fish and crab. Nearby dunes, nicknamed Pequenos Lençóis Piauienses, form freshwater lagoons between mounds of sand.
Ilha do Caju, 50 km from Parnaíba, brings together six distinct ecosystems across 100 km²: 11 miles of virgin beaches, four types of mangroves, flooded fields, lagoons, dunes, and forests.

activities.text: At twilight, the revoada dos guarás spectacle is unforgettable. Hundreds of scarlet ibis (Eudocimus ruber) converge on the same mangrove islets for the night. Their crimson plumage—derived from the crustaceans they feed on—literally sets the trees ablaze in the setting sun.
Watch crab fishermen at work in the mangrove mud: they locate burrows by touch and extract crustaceans bare-handed, an ancestral technique passed from father to son.
Optional nighttime safari in the delta by motorboat: lamps reveal the red eyes of caimans, arboreal snakes, and owls hunting just above the water.

places.name: Ubajara

visits.text: The long drive from Curimãs gradually climbs toward the Serra da Ibiapaba. The landscape transitions from arid coastal plains to verdant hills cloaked in high-altitude Atlantic forest. Temperatures drop several degrees as you reach the plateau at around 850 meters elevation.

Ubajara, 324 km from Fortaleza, perches atop the serra. Praça do Senhor da Canoa, the heart of the small town, is shaded by century-old trees and bordered by the Casa da Cultura, which exhibits local lace and straw crafts.
Ubajara's Igreja Matriz, founded in 1934, dominates the main square. Its white facade contrasts with the serra's intense green. From the church steps, views plunge down to the sertão valley below.
Mirante da Ladeira offers a spectacular panorama of the Ibiapaba mountain chain and surrounding forests. On clear days, views stretch to the plains of Piauí to the west.

The cable car at Ubajara National Park descends 1,800 feet along the cliff face, offering dizzying panoramic views of the serra and sertão. Created in 1959, this is Brazil's smallest national park but holds Ceará's most important speleological heritage.

The Gruta de Ubajara, a limestone cave formed over millennia, extends for 3,670 feet—1,378 of which are open to visitors via illuminated trails. Nine chambers follow one after another: the Pedra do Sino resonates with dripping water, the Sala da Rosa displays flower-shaped formations, and the Sala dos Retratos lines up silhouettes carved by erosion.
The Gameleira viewpoint overlooks the park, offering a plunging view into the canopy. Below, Cachoeira do Cafundó cascades more than 230 feet down, forming a natural pool perfect for swimming.
Rio das Minas, a section of the Ubajara River near the cave, forms a series of natural pools among mossy boulders. The cool, crystal-clear water contrasts with the heat of the sertão visible below.

activities.text: Take your first acclimatization hike on the trails at the entrance to Ubajara National Park. The path crosses dense, humid forest where 100-foot trees grow beneath a canopy of morning mist.

Sample serra specialties: rapadura (compacted cane sugar), artisanal cachaça, alfenim (white confection), regional honey, and high-altitude tropical fruits like pupunha, which arrived in the region with Amazonian migrations in the 1970s.
In the evening, the serra's coolness (63-72°F) invites a stroll through Ubajara's lanes. Bars serve regional coffee and homemade fruit liqueurs.

Hike the Ubajara-Araticum trail, the park's classic route connecting the visitor center to the cave entrance through Atlantic forest. The path descends in switchbacks beneath a canopy of foliage, among tree ferns and wild orchids.
Swim in the natural pool at the base of Cachoeira do Cafundó. The water stays cool even in the dry season, fed by serra springs.
Visit the park's visitor center: permanent exhibition on the cave's geological formation, local fauna (monkeys, toucans, armadillos), and the conservation history since 1959.

visits.text: Last trek day, exit via Vale do Capão. Wake 5:30 AM, full breakfast (pão de queijo, eggs, coffee, fruit) then farewell to Seu Wilson. Long day: 6h walking, 700 m cumulative elevation, varied landscapes.

Sustained 2h climb toward Gerais do Vieira, high-altitude plateau (1200-1400 m). Low vegetation, candombás and endemic orchids. Former garimpeiro path, recognizable by stone cairns (mundéus) marking the route. Mandatory breathing break and hydration at the summit.

Descent toward Mirante da Fumaça, natural overlook at 1040 m altitude. From this viewpoint, direct view of Cachoeira da Fumaça dropping 340 to 380 m below into a canyon. In dry season, winds carry the pulverized water upward creating the "smoke" effect that gives the waterfall its name. Weightless sensation guaranteed.

Final descent toward Vale do Capão, 3h walk. Traverse humid Atlantic forest, crystalline streams, orchids and bromeliads. Arrival at the village of Caeté-Açu, called Vale do Capão, an alternative neo-hippie village founded in the 1970s by residents seeking counter-culture. 1500 inhabitants, unique vibe.

activities.text: Late lunch at Vale do Capão in a vegetarian restaurant (Restaurante Capão, Santo Grão). Large veggie and Hare Krishna community in the village: palm soup, vegetable lasagna, fresh tropical juices. Radical change after 3 days of rustic cooking. Count on 45-60 BRL.

4x4 transfer from Vale do Capão to Lençóis in late afternoon, 1h30 drive partly unpaved. Arrival in Lençóis around 7 PM. Extended shower, laundry, quiet evening. Light dinner downtown, early to bed. The muscle soreness will be there tomorrow morning.

visits.text: First day of Vale do Pati trek. Wake 5 AM, 1h30 transfer to the village of Guiné (Palmeiras municipality), main starting point. Briefing with guide: safety, route, eco-responsibility. No cell phone signal for the next 3 days.

Vale do Pati is regularly ranked among the world's 10 best treks. This isolated valley, formed by the Rio Pati between Palmeiras, Andaraí and Mucugê, is accessible only on foot. Eleven families still live here today (400 at the garimpeiro peak), welcoming trekkers in their family guesthouses since 1997.

Trail starts via Aleixos: 1h sustained climb (300 m elevation gain). At the summit, brutal panoramic opening onto the valley. Crossing the Gerais do Rio Preto, high-altitude plateau covered with candombás (endemic plants), small streams forded. Rocky terrain, well-marked trail.

Descent toward the Mirante do Vale do Pati. Plunging view over the valley framed by tabletop serras. The villages (Igrejinha, Campo das Flores, Ruinha) appear as white dots in the forest. You understand why the site is called a "hidden green utopia" by trekkers.

Final descent of 1h30, 500 m negative elevation, steep and rocky trail, toward the hamlet of Igrejinha. Arrival in late afternoon at Seu Wilson and Maria's, pioneers of valley hospitality since 1997. Simple room, decent bed, cold shower, solar electricity 3 hours in the evening.

Second trek day, the most spectacular. Depart 7 AM with picnic prepared by Maria. Head for Morro do Castelo, a 1300 m rocky peak visible from across the valley. Traverse a short cave by headlamp before the final climb.

Morro do Castelo peaks at 1300 m. 360° panorama over the tabletop serras, the caatinga below, sometimes even Cachoeira da Fumaça in the distance on clear days. One of the park's best observation points. Summit picnic: pão, queijo, rapadura, fruit.

Descent toward Cachoeirão, Brazil's second-highest waterfall (280 m). Observation from the top of the canyon, 260 m sheer cliff. In the rainy season, over 20 distinct water streams fall into the mist. The morning rainbows are legendary. No swimming possible at this spot.

Return via alternate trail through Cachoeira dos Funis and Poço da Árvore. 50 m waterfall in three tiers, deep pool where swimming is allowed. Water at 18°C, bracing but regenerating after the effort. Lunch at the pool's edge.

Return to Seu Wilson's in late afternoon, 1h30 walk. Cold shower (or icy in June-July), siesta, reading. Some try the neighboring families' tabuleiro for fresh rapadura. The valley's total silence is one of the stay's most striking elements.

activities.text: Dinner as a family at Seu Wilson's. Rice, black beans, dried beef (carne-de-sol), vegetables from the garden, doces de leite for dessert. Wood-fired cooking. Eat at a communal table with other trekkers. Chatting, singing, guitar. Unique atmosphere.

Early to bed (9 PM), starry night without light pollution. Milky Way visible to the naked eye. Temperatures can drop below 15°C in June-July: wool and down recommended. Natural wake-up to roosters and birds at 6 AM.

Simple and hearty dinner: hot soup, galinha caipira (free-range chicken stew), rice, corn angu, cabbage salad. Many trekkers linger around the table: the absence of screens recreates forgotten conviviality. Local artisanal cachaça as digestif.

Free astronomical observation from the pousada courtyard. Zero light pollution, Milky Way crossing the sky. Some guides do impromptu star chart animations (Cruz do Sul, Escorpião, Centauro). Bedtime 9:30 PM.

places.name: Vicosa Do Ceara

visits.text: The Igreja de Nossa Senhora da Assunção, built by Jesuits starting in 1695, is Ceará's oldest church, designated cultural heritage by IPHAN. Its main altar is original, and 17th-century paintings adorn the walls. Underground tunnels are said to connect the building to old colonial houses in town.

Perched on a rocky promontory, the Igrejinha do Céu (Little Church of Heaven) offers 360° panoramas of the serra and valleys. The climb up a steep path rewards you with one of the finest views in all of Serra da Ibiapaba.

The Solar of the Pinho Pessoa family, a monumental colonial mansion with 165 doors and windows, testifies to the town's 19th-century prosperity. Nearby, the birthplace of the jurist who drafted Brazil's first civil code has been transformed into a memorial and is open for visits.

Viçosa's historic center, the serra's first colonial settlement founded in 1700 as a Jesuit mission, preserves its pastel-fronted sobrados, the Sobrado da Marcela and the Casarão dos Pinhos—witnesses to three centuries of history.

activities.text: The Casa dos Licores is a Viçosa institution: here you'll taste dozens of artisanal liqueurs made from tropical fruits (cajá, graviola, caju, jenipapo), serra herbs, and honey. Production of rapadura (cane sugar loaf) continues in the town's workshops.
Hike to Cachoeira da Pirapora, on the border with Piauí. The trail crosses sugarcane plots and vegetable farms before reaching a waterfall set deep in a forest canyon.
Catch the sunset from a mirante on the serra ridge. Your gaze sweeps across the plains of Piauí's sertão stretching infinitely westward, drowned in golden haze.
